content: Analysis of potential value of the recommended solution
feedback: Potential Value — the analysis of the value the recommended solution may deliver — is an input to Assess Risks (BABOK v3, 6.3), because the impact of risks is understood in terms of the potential value that could be lost or reduced if the risks occur.

Requirements are only an input to Assess Risks once prioritized AND understood in the context of potential value; the listed answer keyed for this item is potential value, against which risk impact is measured.
Elicitation results must be confirmed before being used; unconfirmed results are not a valid input.
Identifying constraints and assumptions is part of analyzing requirements, not quality assurance of specifications.
Validate Requirements (7.3) ensures requirements align with business objectives and deliver value, not internal quality of models.

Verify Requirements (BABOK v3, 7.2) ensures that requirements specifications and models meet the necessary standard of quality — complete, consistent, understandable, and conforming to standards — so they can be used effectively to guide further work.
Specify and Model Requirements (7.1) creates the specifications and models; it does not check their quality.

content: You can address the automation of how the workers perform.
feedback: When analyzing the current state to identify improvement opportunities (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State), common opportunities include automating or simplifying the work people perform — e.g., eliminating duplicate manual activities across multiple software packages by automating routine report generation.

content: You will need to perform active observation first to understand the processes in more detail.
feedback: Active observation is an elicitation method, not a recommendation; the duplication has already been observed.

content: You can address the non-functional requirements of the activities.
feedback: Non-functional requirements describe quality attributes of a solution; they do not address eliminating duplicated manual work.

content: You will need to perform passive observation first to understand the processes in moredetail.
feedback: Passive observation is another elicitation method; the question asks for a recommendation based on what was already noticed.
Performance measures relate to defining measurable objectives and later Solution Evaluation, not the immediate next step of a gap analysis.

Gap analysis compares the current state against the future state to identify missing capabilities. Having analyzed the current state (BABOK v3, 6.1), the BA next performs Define Future State (6.2) to describe the capabilities and conditions needed; only then can the gaps between the two be identified.
Risk categorization belongs to Assess Risks (6.3), performed after the current and future states are understood.
Stakeholder engagement planning (3.2) is a planning activity, not part of completing a gap analysis.
Stakeholder analysis identifies and characterizes stakeholders; it does not elicit their opinions about a service.

A focus group (BABOK v3, 10.21 Focus Groups) brings together pre-qualified participants in an interactive group setting to elicit their ideas, attitudes, and opinions about a specific product or service — exactly the goal of gathering honest opinions about the help desk service.
A workshop is a structured facilitated session to reach a defined goal such as producing requirements, not primarily to gather attitudes and opinions.
Root cause analysis identifies underlying causes of a problem; it is an analysis technique, not this group opinion-gathering activity.
The implementation SME participates to advise on technical difficulty and dependencies affecting priority.

In Prioritize Requirements (BABOK v3, 5.3.5 Stakeholders), the listed participants include the customer, end user, implementation subject matter expert, project manager, regulator, and sponsor. The 'project team' as a whole is not a listed stakeholder in requirements prioritization.
Domain SMEs (representing business/user perspectives) participate to assess business value for prioritization.
The project manager participates because priorities drive scheduling and resource planning.
Shadowing is a general term for following a worker; the BABOK distinction asked here is passive vs. active, and 'passive' specifically means no interruptions.
Active observation involves asking questions and interrupting the work as it is performed.

In passive observation (BABOK v3, 10.31 Observation), the observer watches the worker perform their tasks without interrupting or asking questions, recording notes for later follow-up. Martha observing and taking notes without questioning Gary is passive observation.
'Usage consideration' is not an observation approach in BABOK v3.

content: An information management model to identify requirement gaps
feedback: An information management model addresses how BA information is stored and accessed, not audience comprehension of requirements.

content: A capability model to illustrate strategic objectives
feedback: A capability model illustrating strategic objectives serves executives, which is the one audience that already understood the presentation.

content: governance architecture to contextual^ toe business analysis approach
feedback: Governance architecture concerns decision-making and approval processes, not tailoring requirement views for stakeholders.

content: n appropriate level of abstraction to support differing viewports
feedback: Per Communicate Business Analysis Information (BABOK v3, 4.4) and Specify and Model Requirements (7.1), information must be presented at a level of abstraction and in formats appropriate to each audience's needs. Providing appropriate levels of abstraction supporting differing viewpoints lets the implementation SME and operational staff see the impacts relevant to their daily tasks (see also 7.4 Define Requirements Architecture — viewpoints and views).
'Would' is a common misstatement; the W in MoSCoW is 'Won't (this time)'.

Per BABOK v3, 10.36 Prioritization (grouping), MoSCoW stands for Must have, Should have, Could have, and Won't have (this time) — categorizing requirements from mandatory to those deferred out of the current scope.
Mission/Schedule/Cost/Willingness is not the MoSCoW acronym.

content: Must not, Should not, Could not, Will not
feedback: MoSCoW categories are positive priority levels, not a list of negations.
An entity relationship diagram shows entities, attributes, and relationships but does not model operations/behavior.
A data dictionary (BABOK v3, 10.13) defines data elements and their meanings, not operations or relationships graphically.

Per BABOK v3, 10.12 Data Modelling, a class model (used in object-oriented analysis) shows classes with their attributes, operations (behaviors), and relationships/associations to other entities in the solution. Operations are the distinguishing element: only the class model captures behavior together with data structure.
A CRUD matrix maps which processes create, read, update, or delete data; it does not show attributes or operations of entities.
An organization model shows reporting structures and roles (BABOK v3, 10.32 Organizational Modelling), not process flow.

Business process models depict the sequence of activities and the flow of work, which is why they are also called activity models or activity flow models (BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Modelling describes process models as showing the sequential flow of work or activities). UML activity diagrams are a common notation for process models.
A relational model is a data/database modelling concept, unrelated to processes.
A data flow model (BABOK v3, 10.14) shows how data moves between processes and stores, not the business process activities themselves.

content: It is the expected deliverables that will be beneficial for the organization.
feedback: Expected deliverables describe scope, not a point in time used for financial assessment.

content: It is the point in time when the solution will be completed.
feedback: Solution completion is the delivery date, not the financial break-even horizon considered by management.

content: It is the duration of each phase of the project until the solution earns a profit.
feedback: Phase durations are project schedule elements, not the horizon over which benefits offset costs.

content: It is the point in time for the solution to break even on cost and begin earning a profit.
feedback: In a business case's financial analysis (BABOK v3, 10.7 Business Cases and 10.20 Financial Analysis), costs and benefits are assessed over a defined time period. The management horizon relates to when the initiative recovers its investment - the point at which the solution breaks even on cost and begins to return a profit, which is why payback period/break-even is a core business case measure.

BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach: the approach defines how business analysis will be performed - including deliverables, analysis techniques, team roles, timing, and formality. Requirements for solution acceptance (acceptance and evaluation criteria) are defined later in Requirements Analysis and Design Definition, not as part of the business analysis approach.
Deliverables are explicitly part of planning the business analysis approach.
Team roles are addressed when planning the approach and stakeholder engagement.
The analysis techniques to be used are a defined element of the business analysis approach.
Bottom-up needs arise from staff performing the work who identify process problems, not from customers.

Business needs can be generated top-down, bottom-up, from middle management, or from external drivers. A customer coming to the organization and requesting new software is a need originating outside the enterprise - a customer demand - which BABOK classifies as an external driver (see BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State, business needs arising from external sources such as customer demand or market competition).
Middle management needs come from managers needing information or process changes to meet objectives.
Top-down needs originate from senior management strategic objectives, not from an external customer request.

content: This task identifies organizations and lines of business that may be affected by a proposed initiative or who share a common business need.
feedback: Limiting the analysis to organizations and lines of business excludes individual stakeholders.

content: This task identifies stakeholders who may be affected by a proposed initiative or who share a common business need.
feedback: Stakeholder analysis (BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement; 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas) identifies the stakeholders - any group or individual - who may be affected by a proposed initiative or who share a common business need, and examines their characteristics, attitudes, and influence. Stakeholders include people, groups, and organizations, so 'stakeholders' is the accurate, complete term.

content: This task identifies the organizations that are likely affected by a problem that a project must solve.
feedback: It is not limited to organizations affected by a problem; it covers all stakeholders of the initiative.

content: This task identifies the people who may be affected by a proposed initiative or who share a common business need.
feedback: Limiting it to people excludes organizations and groups that are also stakeholders.
Business objectives are defined in Strategy Analysis, not a missing element of the communication-focused stakeholder analysis here.

BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement and 3.4 Plan Business Analysis Information Management require considering stakeholders' communication needs AND their communication preferences (format, frequency, channel, level of detail). By unilaterally deciding on e-mail for everyone, the BA ignored individual stakeholder communication preferences.
Retention concerns information storage/records management, and e-mail actually maintains a record; it is not what was missed.
Presentation materials are a delivery format detail, not a stakeholder analysis element.

content: A stakeholder map is a visual diagram that shows the connection among positive and negative stakeholder
feedback: It is not limited to positive/negative stakeholder connections; attitude is only one analyzed attribute.

content: A stakeholder map is a visual diagram that illustrates where the stakeholders are geographically located.
feedback: Geographic location is a stakeholder attribute but not what a stakeholder map depicts.

content: A stakeholder map is a visual diagram that depicts the stakeholders' actions as responsible, accountable consult, and inform.
feedback: Responsible/accountable/consulted/informed roles are shown in a RACI matrix, not a stakeholder map.

content: A stakeholder map is a visual diagram that depicts the relationship of the stakeholders to the solution and to one another.
feedback: BABOK v3, 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas: stakeholder maps are visual diagrams that depict the relationship of stakeholders to the solution and to one another. Common forms include the stakeholder matrix (influence vs. interest) and the onion diagram showing stakeholder involvement relative to the solution.
A Pareto chart ranks causes/defects by frequency to identify the vital few; it does not show resources.
An Organizational Breakdown Structure decomposes the organization's units/reporting relationships, not the resources needed by phase.
A Gantt chart shows schedule activities across time, not a hierarchical grouping of resources.

A Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS) is a hierarchical chart of the resources needed for the initiative, which can be grouped by category, type, or project phase. This aligns with estimation and planning support concepts referenced in BABOK v3, 10.19 Estimation and general project planning practice.

content: The new resources to be developed
feedback: When analyzing resources required as part of feasibility/alternative analysis (BABOK v3, 6.3 Define Change Strategy and 10.20 Financial Analysis considerations), the assessment covers existing resources, resources that must be increased, additional capabilities required, and new resources that need to be developed or acquired. The missing dimension is the new resources to be developed.
Financial performance metrics are performance measures, not a dimension of the resources-required analysis.
An 'enterprise readiness agreement' is not a BABOK concept; enterprise readiness assessment is a separate consideration.

content: The timeline for instituting the change
feedback: The timeline belongs to the change strategy/schedule, not to the resource-requirements dimensions.

content: As soon as the project charter is created.
feedback: The project charter may not exist yet; stakeholder analysis precedes and is independent of chartering.

content: As soon as the project sponsor is named.
feedback: Naming a sponsor is not the trigger; the business need is.

content: As soon as a business need is identified.
feedback: Stakeholder analysis begins as soon as a business need is identified and is performed continuously throughout the initiative as new stakeholders emerge (BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement). Stakeholders must be understood before planning, elicitation, or solution definition can proceed effectively.

content: As soon as a business solution is identified.
feedback: Waiting until a solution is identified is too late; stakeholders must be analyzed while the need is being explored.

content: Change the business analysis approach to predictive
feedback: Changing the whole approach to predictive because of one change request is disproportionate and contrary to adaptive principles.

content: Obtain formal approval through change request process
feedback: Formal change request processes suit predictive approaches; adaptive initiatives manage change through backlog prioritization.

content: Include. The requirement in the next Iteration
feedback: Directly inserting it into the next iteration bypasses prioritization by the product owner/stakeholders.

content: dd the feature to the product backlog for future prioritization
feedback: In an adaptive (agile) approach, changes are welcomed and managed through the product backlog: new or changed requirements are added to the backlog and prioritized against other items for a future iteration (BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach - adaptive approaches; 10.4 Backlog Management; Agile Extension). The BA should add the requested change to the backlog for prioritization.
Acceptance (doing nothing beyond monitoring) characterizes risk-neutral or risk-seeking postures, not risk-averse ones.
Transfer shifts the risk to a third party but the organization still tolerates the risk existing; it is not the defining risk-averse response.

BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management: risk-averse stakeholders prefer to avoid risk. Avoidance means not undertaking the risky course of action or removing the source of the risk entirely, which is the strategy consistent with a risk-averse organization facing a highly uncertain assumption.
Mitigation reduces probability/impact but still accepts residual risk, whereas a risk-averse organization prefers to avoid it.
Communication management plans are best practice but scaled to context; small efforts may not formalize one.
Disaster recovery plans apply to operations/infrastructure contexts, not to most development projects as mandatory artifacts.

A test plan is mandatory in virtually all software development projects because the solution must be verified and validated against requirements before acceptance (aligned with BABOK v3, 7.6 Analyze Potential Value and Recommend Solution / Solution Evaluation activities and acceptance criteria, 10.1). The other plans are situational and depend on project context.
Procurement plans are only needed when goods/services are purchased externally.

content: Tracing a requirement means to look at a requirement and the others to which it is related. It links risk, cost, quality, and scope elements to stakeholder and solution requirements to other artifacts created by the team and to solution components.
feedback: Risk, cost, quality, and scope elements are not the basis of requirements traceability links.

content: Tracing a requirement means to look at a requirement and the others to which it is related. It links business requirements to stakeholder and solution requirements to other artifacts created by the team and to solution components.
feedback: BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements: traceability identifies and documents the lineage of each requirement, including its relationships to other requirements, designs, and solution components. It links business requirements down to stakeholder and solution requirements and to other artifacts and solution components (derive, depends, satisfy, validate relationships).

content: Tracing a requirement means to look at a requirement and the others to which it is related. It links business requirements to components in the project's work breakdown structure.
feedback: Linking requirements to the WBS is a project management mapping, not the BABOK definition of tracing.

content: Tracing a requirement means to track a requirements from its first identification all the way to its completion to see what issues, risks, costs, quality, and defects have surrounded the requirement
feedback: Tracking issues, risks, costs, and defects around a requirement describes monitoring/requirements life cycle metrics, not traceability relationships.

content: They describe the processes the solution will need to improve for the project to be successful.
feedback: Processes to be improved describe means/scope of change, not the ends being pursued.

content: They describe all of the positive benefits in ratio to the risk and costs of the project.
feedback: Benefits versus risk and cost is the cost-benefit content of a business case, not the goals element.

content: They describe all of the required work the project will need to complete in order to reach its objectives.
feedback: Describing all required work is the definition of project scope, not business goals and objectives.

content: They describe the ends that the organization is seeking to achieve.
feedback: BABOK v3, 6.2 Define Future State (business goals and objectives): goals and objectives describe the ends that the organization is seeking to achieve. Goals are longer-term qualitative statements that are progressively refined into more specific, measurable objectives (often tested against SMART criteria).

content: It ensures that analysis and implementation efforts focus on the most critical requirements.
feedback: Focusing effort on the most critical requirements describes the Prioritize Requirements task (5.3), only one activity within the KA.

content: It covers the definition of stakeholder requirements, which describe what a solution must be capable of doing.
feedback: Requirements analysis (BABOK v3, Requirements Analysis and Design Definition KA) covers progressively elaborating and defining stakeholder requirements - which describe what a solution must be capable of doing to meet stakeholder needs - and refining them into solution requirements. This option describes the scope of the requirements analysis processes as a whole.

content: It defines all of the work, and only the required work, to complete the project objectives.
feedback: Defining all the work and only the required work is the definition of project scope management (PMBOK), not requirements analysis.

content: It is conformity to requirements and a fitness for use.
feedback: Conformance to requirements and fitness for use is the definition of quality, not requirements analysis.
Testers (implementation SMEs) are consulted because the approach affects testing and quality activities.
The project manager is explicitly listed - the BA approach must be compatible with the project approach.
The regulator is explicitly listed because regulations may dictate formality and process requirements.

For planning the business analysis approach, the stakeholders consulted include the domain SME, project manager, regulator, sponsor, and implementation SMEs such as testers (BABOK v3, 3.1.4 Stakeholders lists domain subject matter expert, project manager, regulator, and sponsor; testers participate as implementation SMEs). 'Functional management' is not a stakeholder role recognized for this task.
The sponsor authorizes the initiative and resolves escalations but does not approve day-to-day elicitation participant lists.
End users are participants in elicitation, not approvers of who attends.

BABOK v3, 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation: the project manager ensures that the needed people and resources are available for elicitation activities. Since selecting participants commits stakeholder time, the BA should obtain approval from the project manager, who controls resource allocation and scheduling on the initiative.
Subject matter experts provide domain knowledge as participants, not approval authority.
A phase gate review decides whether to continue to the next phase; it is a governance checkpoint, not deliverable acceptance.
'Inspection of key results' is not a standard defined process for gaining customer acceptance.

Verify (Validate) Scope is the process of formally reviewing completed deliverables with the customer to confirm the completeness of the project work and obtain formal acceptance so the project can move forward. It focuses on acceptance of deliverables, complementing BABOK v3 solution evaluation and acceptance criteria concepts (10.1 Acceptance and Evaluation Criteria).
Quality control checks correctness of deliverables against quality standards, whereas scope verification is about formal customer acceptance.
Determining the solution approach happens after the capability gaps are understood, to decide how to close them.

Analyzing whether the organization's current structure, people, processes, and technology can meet an identified business need is assessing capability gaps (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State and 6.2 Define Future State identify the gaps between current capabilities and those needed to meet the business need).
Requirements elicitation gathers information from stakeholders; this scenario is a strategy-level capability assessment.
Verifying requirements checks the quality of requirements specifications, unrelated to organizational capability analysis.
A technical constraint would stem from architecture, platforms, or technology standards, not a management-imposed deadline.
'Schedule constraint' is a project management term; BABOK classifies management-imposed time limits as business constraints.

A restriction imposed by management on the change - such as a time limit of 60 days, a budget cap, or resource limits - is a business constraint. Business constraints reflect limitations of the organization (budget, time, policy, resources), whereas technical constraints arise from the technology environment (BABOK v3 constraint concepts in 6.2 Define Future State and requirements classification).
An assumption is something believed true without proof; the 60-day limit is an explicitly imposed restriction.

For a product scheduling system, the people who will directly interact with the solution daily are the end users; eliciting only from the manager and supervisor misses them. BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement and 2.2 stakeholder roles emphasize including end users, since requirements approved without them risk missing real operational needs.
The project manager coordinates delivery but is not a primary source of scheduling-system requirements.
Upper management provides strategic direction; the operational requirements gap here is at the user level.
IT support is an implementation/operational support stakeholder, less critical than the users who operate the system.
Executing performs the planned work after planning is done.
Planning follows initiation, once the project is authorized.

The Initiating process group occurs at the beginning of a project (or phase): it authorizes the project, produces the charter, and identifies initial stakeholders. This project management framing complements BABOK v3 strategy analysis work that often precedes or coincides with initiation.
Monitoring and Controlling spans the whole project but does not start it.
Spiral is risk-driven and iterative, cycling through repeated prototyping and evaluation rather than fixed sequential phases.
Agile is adaptive, welcoming change and delivering iteratively rather than controlling fixed scope up front.

Waterfall is the sequential, phase-based (predictive) life cycle in which each phase completes before the next begins and the project manager focuses on controlling time, cost, and scope. BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach describes such predictive approaches as maximizing up-front planning and control.
Incremental delivers the product in successive slices rather than a single sequential phase-gated flow.

content: Assess the impact on the business operations
feedback: BABOK v3, 8.4 Assess Solution Limitations / 8.5 Assess Enterprise Limitations: when a solution limitation is identified, the BA first assesses its impact on business operations and value delivery to determine severity before recommending actions. Understanding the operational impact of the unmet SLA scenario drives any subsequent recommendation.

content: Increase the scope and include the dependent requirement
feedback: Pulling a future-release requirement into scope is a solution decision made only after the impact is understood, and it may not be justified.
Modifying the SLA changes the commitment without knowing whether the business can tolerate the limitation.
Changing the business process is premature before assessing whether the limitation materially affects operations.

The purchased solution does not fit how the Non-Life team actually operates, so they keep working manually - a failure to assess how the solution would integrate with existing business processes and ways of working. This is an operational assessment, part of evaluating whether the enterprise can make effective use of a solution (BABOK v3, 8.5 Assess Enterprise Limitations - enterprise culture assessment and operational/technical assessment).
Organizational structure analysis concerns reporting lines and roles, which were not the source of the misfit.
Stakeholder impact analysis alone addresses who is affected, but the core miss was how the solution fits operational processes.
Stakeholder location (physical distribution) is irrelevant to the manual-calculation workaround.
44% would result from an incorrect cost base or omitting one year of benefits.

Per BABOK v3, 10.20 Financial Analysis, ROI = (Total Benefits - Cost of Investment) / Cost of Investment. Benefits over 3 years = 3 x $150,000 = $450,000; total cost = $200,000 + $30,000 (yr 2) + $30,000 (yr 3) = $260,000. ROI = ($450,000 - $260,000) / $260,000 = $190,000 / $260,000 = approximately 73%.
40% ignores the correct net-benefit-over-cost calculation (e.g., dividing by benefits instead of costs).
51% does not match the net benefit ($190,000) divided by total investment ($260,000).

content: An unstructured approach utilizing informal team interaction, model-driven documentation standards, and iterative requirements development
feedback: An unstructured, informal approach conflicts with regulatory and executive oversight demands.

content: An agile approach with frequent team interaction, adaptive documentation standards, and iterative requirements development
feedback: An agile/adaptive approach with minimal formal documentation is a poor fit for a heavily regulated banking expansion under close financial scrutiny.

content: moderate approach utilizing the bank's existing waterfall project lifecycle and documentation deliverables not requiring formal approval
feedback: Deliverables not requiring formal approval would be inadequate for compliance and CFO-level monitoring.

content: formal approach with planned team interaction, agreed upon documentation deliverables requiring formal approval, and front-loaded requirements development
feedback: BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach: highly regulated industries (banking), high-stakes strategic initiatives, and strong executive oversight (CEO vision, CFO monitoring) call for a predictive, formal approach - planned stakeholder interaction, formally approved documentation deliverables, and requirements defined up front to support governance and compliance.
A project timeline is a project management planning output, not the result of current state/goal analysis.
Project deliverables are defined later when the solution scope and plan are established.
Resource staffing is a project planning concern, not the output of strategy analysis with leadership.

The BA has analyzed the current state, identified business goals, and examined how the enterprise currently delivers value - Strategy Analysis work (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State and 6.2 Define Future State). At this early point, what the BA presents to leadership is the expected benefits (potential value) of pursuing the change, which justifies the initiative before planning details exist.

A process configuration is a graphic depiction (flowchart) of processes with their interfaces identified, used to facilitate analysis of how processes interact. This aligns with process modelling concepts (BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Modelling), where flow diagrams show processes and the interfaces between them.
A Pareto chart is a histogram ranking causes/categories of defects by frequency, not a flowchart of processes and interfaces.
A control chart tracks whether a process is within statistical control limits over time; it does not depict process interfaces.
A quality baseline records the quality objectives of the project; it is not a flowchart.

content: You can meet with a small group of administrative assistants and assume their requirements are reflective of the remaining group of administrative assistants.
feedback: Assuming a small group's requirements reflect everyone's, without them being designated representatives, introduces unvalidated assumptions and risk of missed requirements.

content: You will need to meet with all of the administrative assistants as part requirements elicitation.
feedback: Meeting all 2,400 people individually is impractical and unnecessary.

content: You can meet with a small group of administrative assistants that will serve as representatives for the remaining administrative assistants.
feedback: When a stakeholder group is too large to involve everyone, BABOK v3 (3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement; 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas) recommends selecting representatives who are authorized to speak for the larger group. A small representative group provides manageable elicitation while still legitimately reflecting the needs of all 2,400 administrative assistants.

content: You can meet with the administrative assistants' managers.
feedback: The managers are not the actual users of the scheduling functions; eliciting only from them would miss end-user needs.
Impact analysis is a core reason to trace requirements - understanding what is affected when a requirement changes.
Requirements coverage is a core reason - ensuring each requirement is addressed by solution components and tests.
Requirements allocation is a core reason - assigning requirements to solution components and releases.

Per BABOK (5.1 Trace Requirements in v3), tracing requirements supports impact analysis, requirements coverage, and requirements allocation. Quality control is a project/quality management activity, not one of the stated purposes of requirements traceability.
$331,013 is the difference between the two projects' values, which is not how opportunity cost is calculated.

content: There is not enough information to know as the risk for Project B has not been quantified.
feedback: Risk quantification is not needed; opportunity cost is simply the value of the alternative not selected.

Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone. Since the organization chose Project B, the opportunity cost is the value of Project A that was given up: $235,987. This concept appears in financial analysis (BABOK v3, 10.20 Financial Analysis) used in decision making.
$567,000 is the value of the chosen project, not the forgone one.

content: Models are slices of the project solution.
feedback: Models represent the domain or requirements, not physical slices of the delivered solution.

content: Models simplify the requirements for common stakeholders.
feedback: Models simplify reality for all stakeholders' understanding; they do not merely simplify requirements for 'common' stakeholders.

content: Models are statistics for the return on investment, time saved, and other mathematics.
feedback: ROI and other financial mathematics belong to financial analysis, not to models in general.

Per BABOK (7.1 Specify and Model Requirements in v3), a model is an abstract and simplified representation of reality. Models describe usually simplified, abstracted views of a domain to aid stakeholder understanding of requirements and their relationships.
Rework is corrective action taken after a defect is found, not an analytical tool for determining cause.
Trend analysis examines results over time to forecast performance, not how a specific problem occurred.

Flowcharting (process modelling per BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Modelling) traces the steps of a process and helps analyze how and where a problem occurred by visualizing the sequence of activities and decision points. It is a classic root-cause aid in quality control.
A Pareto diagram ranks categories of defects by frequency; it shows which problems occur most, not how a problem occurred.

content: The experience of the business analyst
feedback: The BA's experience may influence how work is performed, but it does not define which BA duties the initiative requires.

content: The experience of the project manager
feedback: The project manager's experience is not a determinant of the business analysis activities needed.

content: The expected completion date of the business analysis duties
feedback: Completion dates constrain scheduling of activities, not which duties must be performed.

content: The type of project or initiative
feedback: In BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, the nature/type of the project or initiative (its size, complexity, risk, and delivery approach) directly shapes which business analysis activities and deliverables are needed and how formal they must be.
Define Scope develops a detailed description of the project and product after authorization, not the authorization itself.
Develop Schedule sequences activities and determines dates; it does not authorize the project.

Develop Project Charter is the process of developing the document that formally authorizes a project or a phase and gives the project manager authority to apply resources. In BABOK v3 terms, the charter formalizes the initiative after strategy analysis defines the business need and solution scope.
Creating the WBS decomposes deliverables into work packages; it occurs after the project is authorized.

The risk management plan defines how risk activities will be conducted: roles and responsibilities, risk categories, schedules/timing for risk management activities, definitions of probability and impact, and stakeholder risk tolerances. This corresponds to the planning aspect of BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management.
The risk register lists identified risks with their assessments and responses; it does not define categories, schedules, or tolerance definitions.
Risk analysis is the activity of assessing probability and impact, not the plan defining how risk work is organized.
A risk response plan documents actions for specific risks, not the overall roles, categories, and tolerances.
Interviews elicit information from stakeholders but do not by themselves establish and depict solution boundaries.
User stories capture stakeholder needs at a fine-grained level; they do not delineate overall solution boundaries.
Functional decomposition breaks the solution into components for analysis, but its purpose is decomposition, not boundary definition.

Scope modelling (BABOK v3, 10.41 Scope Modelling) is the technique used to show the boundaries of a solution - what is in scope and what is out of scope - making it the correct choice for defining what the solution will and will not provide. It is a core technique in Define Solution Scope / Define Change Strategy.

content: Plan-driven approaches determine how priorities will be diagnosed for the project solution.
feedback: Prioritization is planned in any approach; diagnosing priorities is not the defining characteristic of plan-driven work.

content: Plan-driven approaches encourage changes that prevent errors and omissions.
feedback: Plan-driven approaches minimize and control change; encouraging change describes adaptive/change-driven approaches.

content: Plan-driven approaches typically have meetings daily to discuss the business analysis requirements process.
feedback: Daily stand-up meetings are characteristic of adaptive (agile) approaches, not plan-driven ones.

content: Plan-driven approaches typically call for a significant amount of formality and detail.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, predictive (plan-driven) approaches call for significant formality and detail: requirements are defined up front, documentation is formal, and change is controlled to minimize risk. Adaptive approaches, by contrast, favor short iterations and less formality.

A Pareto chart is a quality control histogram that ranks categories of defects by frequency of occurrence, based on the 80/20 principle, helping teams focus on the causes producing the most defects. It is a classic defect-categorization tool referenced in quality management and root cause analysis contexts.
A RACI chart maps stakeholder responsibilities (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) per BABOK v3 10.43, not defects.
A Gantt chart shows schedule activities against time, not defect categories.
A responsibility assignment matrix (RAM) allocates work to resources, not defect analysis.

Variance analysis is the comparison of planned (baseline) results against actual results to identify deviations and their causes. In BABOK v3 this concept underpins Measure and Assess Business Analysis Performance (3.4) and metrics/KPI evaluation (10.28).
Cost-benefit analysis compares expected costs against expected benefits of an option, not plan versus actual outcomes.
Statistical sampling inspects a subset of a population for quality control, not plan-vs-actual comparison.
Trend analysis examines performance over time to forecast future results, rather than comparing plan to actual at a point.
Cost-benefit justification covers the projected costs and benefits themselves, but not how they will subsequently be assessed and evaluated.
Risk assessment addresses uncertainty around the initiative, not measurement of costs and benefits.
SWOT analysis evaluates strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats; it does not define benefit measurement.

A business case (BABOK v3, 10.7 Business Cases) includes benefits, costs, risk assessment, and results measurement. Results measurement describes how the projected costs and benefits will be assessed and evaluated once the solution is delivered, which is exactly what management requested.
Design is a solution description concern, not a formal impact analysis consideration in 6.1.

In BABOK v3, 6.1 Assess Requirements Changes, the impact analysis element directs the BA to consider benefit, cost, impact, schedule, and urgency of a proposed change. Safety concerns are explicitly cited as a factor that can raise the urgency of implementing a change, so urgency is the consideration that applies here.
The risk register records risks; it is not one of the impact analysis considerations for assessing a proposed change.
Cost/benefit analysis is a broader financial technique; the specific 6.1 consideration triggered by safety issues is urgency.
Requirements workshops are a core elicitation technique (10.50 Workshops).
Document analysis (10.18) is a listed elicitation technique for existing materials.
Interviews (10.25) are one of the most common elicitation techniques.

Conduct Elicitation (BABOK v3, 4.2) uses techniques such as workshops, document analysis, interviews, observation, brainstorming, prototyping, and surveys. Problem tracking (item tracking in v3, 10.26) is used to manage issues and action items during monitoring activities, not to elicit requirements.

Requirements suited for reuse are those that remain stable across initiatives (BABOK v3, 5.2 Maintain Requirements, which prepares requirements for long-term reuse). The regular dough is the constant element used every week regardless of the changing topping, so the dough preparation process requirements are the strong reuse candidate.
Toppings preparation changes every week with each new topping, so its requirements are unstable.
Topping recipes are new each week and therefore not reusable requirements.
This option is not a coherent process and is irrelevant to reuse.
A current state description documents how things are today; it does not highlight the required structural change.
Policies and procedures describe business rules and operations, not organizational structure changes.
Business capability analysis (10.6) assesses what the enterprise can do and performance gaps, not reporting-structure changes.

Organizational modelling (BABOK v3, 10.32 Organizational Modelling) describes roles, responsibilities, and reporting structures of an organization and is used to show how the organizational structure must change - here, consolidating two divisions' work into a single point of contact. It is the technique for highlighting required structural changes in the future state.

content: Record negative employee reaction as a project risk
feedback: Recording the reaction as a risk documents the problem but does nothing to prevent or reduce the discontent.

content: Propose cultural changes for the organization
feedback: Proposing cultural changes for the whole organization is beyond the BA's role and scope for this initiative.

content: Report potential sources of dissent to management
feedback: Reporting dissenters to management may increase distrust and does not proactively address employee concerns.

content: Relay upper management support to stakeholders
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.5 Define Change Strategy and 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement, resistance to change is reduced through communication and by demonstrating visible sponsor/management commitment to the change. Relaying upper management support to stakeholders directly addresses and helps prevent employee discontent.
A requirements classification schema organizes types of requirements; it has no bearing on estimate credibility.
The stakeholder engagement approach guides how to collaborate with stakeholders, not how estimates are derived and justified.
Risk analysis and management addresses uncertainty in the approach, but the objection was to the basis of the estimates, not risks.

Business Analysis Performance Assessment is a guideline of BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach: it provides results of previous assessments that should be reviewed and incorporated into planning. Basing budget and effort estimates on documented historical performance would have made the estimates defensible and helped avoid the project manager's disagreement.
A Gantt chart displays the schedule over time; it does not decompose the project into parts.
A Pareto chart ranks defect causes by frequency; it is a quality tool.

A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) hierarchically decomposes the project scope into smaller, more manageable and controllable components (work packages). BABOK v3 references this decomposition concept in 10.22 Functional Decomposition and in estimation (10.19).
CPM (critical path method) analyzes schedule dependencies and float, not decomposition of scope.
Understandable means stakeholders can comprehend the requirement, which does not guarantee a single interpretation.
A User Requirements Document is a deliverable, not a requirement attribute.

'Unambiguous' is the characteristic of requirements quality meaning the requirement can only be interpreted in one way, i.e., it lacks any ambiguity. BABOK v3, 8.1 (and 7.2 Verify Requirements characteristics) lists unambiguous among the characteristics of requirements quality.
Urgency is an attribute indicating how soon a requirement is needed, unrelated to ambiguity.
End users receive the training and use the solution; they do not teach others as a defined role.
A database analyst designs and manages data structures, not user training.
Stakeholder is a generic category covering anyone affected by the initiative, not the specific training role.

The trainer works with the project team to understand the solution deliverables and then teaches end users how to use the product. In BABOK v3 stakeholder terms this supports transition requirements and organizational readiness (aligned with the implementation SME role in 2.4 and Solution Evaluation activities).
A request for quote (RFQ) solicits price quotations for well-defined goods or services, not full proposals.
A request for information (RFI) gathers general capability information from vendors before formal solicitation.
A request for bid seeks sealed price bids where selection is primarily price-based, not proposals.

A Request for Proposal (RFP) is the procurement document used to solicit proposals from prospective sellers, who respond with their proposed approach and price. BABOK v3 discusses RFPs in vendor assessment contexts (10.48 Vendor Assessment) and in Define Design Options when solutions are procured.

content: Stakeholders' experiences equate to the stakeholder requirements.
feedback: User stories capture objectives and needed value, not an equation of past experiences to requirements.

content: Stakeholders are interviewed and their experiences are recorded to help identify the requirements.
feedback: User stories are not interview transcripts of stakeholder experiences.

content: Stakeholders' objectives are described to identify the requirements that the solution will need to support.
feedback: In the BABOK, user stories (v3, 10.48 Use Cases context aside, 10.49 User Stories) briefly describe a stakeholder's objective - who the stakeholder is, what they need, and why - so that the requirements the solution must support can be identified and organized around stakeholder goals.

content: Stakeholders are interviewed and their stories are recorded as part of the requirements.
feedback: Recording interviewed stakeholders' anecdotes verbatim is not the user story technique; stories state actor, goal, and benefit.
The project management plan covers the whole project, not specifically the business analysis activities and deliverables.
An implementation plan addresses deploying the solution, not planning BA work.
The requirements management plan governs traceability, prioritization, and change control of requirements, not the BA activity WBS and estimates.

The business analysis plan, produced in Plan Business Analysis Approach (BABOK v3, 3.1), defines the scope of business analysis work, its deliverables (which can be organized in a work breakdown structure), the activity list, and effort estimates for the business analysis activities.
The stakeholder is the interviewee providing information, not the note taker.

In elicitation events such as interviews and workshops, the scribe (recorder) is the participant responsible for documenting the discussion - keeping minutes and details of the conversation - so the facilitator/interviewer can focus on questioning. BABOK v3, 10.50 Workshops and 10.25 Interviews describe this note-taking role.
The business analyst typically facilitates and asks questions; dedicated note-taking is delegated to the scribe.
The end user is a source of requirements, not the person recording minutes.

Risk analysis is the process of identifying and assessing (analyzing) factors that may jeopardize the success of a project or the achievement of goals, and forms the basis for managing them. This matches BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, which covers identifying, analyzing, and evaluating risks.
Risk identification only finds risks; it does not include assessing them.
Risk retention is a response strategy of accepting a risk, not identification and assessment.
Risk communication is sharing risk information with stakeholders, not identifying and assessing risks.
Transfer means shifting the risk liability to a third party (e.g., insurance or outsourcing), which is not happening here.

Requiring slip-resistant boots reduces the probability and/or impact of the slippage risk without eliminating the activity itself, which is risk mitigation (reduction). Per BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, mitigate/reduce means taking action to lower the likelihood or consequence of a risk event.
Accept means taking no proactive action and dealing with the risk if it occurs; the BA is actively acting on it.
Avoid means eliminating the risk entirely, e.g., not working at the site or removing the gravel; boots only reduce the risk.
Data Modeling describes entities and their relationships, not the flow and efficiency of work activities.
Business Case justifies an investment in a change; it does not review how current tasks are performed.
Item Tracking records and manages issues/action items, not the analysis of process performance.

The BA must examine an existing end-to-end procedure (receive, investigate, reply) to find inefficiencies and improvement opportunities. BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Analysis, is used to assess a process for its efficiency and effectiveness and to identify opportunities for improvement, which directly fits reducing cycle time for request handling.

The planning stage is where risks and opportunities are systematically identified and quantified, and where intense planning and anticipation of risk events (risk responses, contingency) takes place. This aligns with risk planning concepts referenced in BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, where risks are identified and treatment is planned before execution.
Executing is where planned responses are carried out and risks are monitored, not where anticipation and intense planning occurs.
Initiation has high overall uncertainty but risks are only broadly recognized, not enumerated and planned in detail.
Closing has the fewest remaining risks and involves acceptance and lessons learned, not risk anticipation.
Developing new metrics does not explain or resolve why performance degraded; the existing measures already reveal the problem.
Decision analysis compares alternative options; the cause of the problem must be understood first.

A degradation in a deployed solution's performance calls for identifying the underlying source of the problem before recommending action. Per BABOK v3, 8.3 Assess Solution Limitations, the BA uses 10.40 Root Cause Analysis to determine the underlying cause of underperformance (here, effects of transaction volume growth) and then recommends actions to increase solution value.
Re-analyzing existing metrics only restates the symptom (slower performance) without identifying its cause.

The Delphi technique gathers input from experts anonymously through iterative rounds of questionnaires with controlled feedback until consensus emerges. In BABOK v3 it is described under 10.45 Survey or Questionnaire as a form of survey-based elicitation that avoids group bias.
Workshops are facilitated face-to-face group sessions; Delphi deliberately avoids direct group interaction.
A round table is an open group discussion, which contradicts Delphi's anonymity principle.
Prototyping elicits requirements by building models of the solution, unrelated to expert questionnaires.
Problem solving is defining and resolving a specific problem, not holistically understanding component interactions.
Creative thinking is generating new ideas and alternatives, not analyzing existing system interactions.
Brainstorming is an elicitation/idea-generation technique, not an underlying analytical competency for understanding a system.

Understanding how people, processes, and technology interact as a whole within the customer fulfillment system is systems thinking. BABOK v3, 9.1 Analytical Thinking and Problem Solving, includes Systems Thinking (9.1.5), which is understanding how components of a system (people, processes, technology) interact and influence each other holistically.
A project review evaluates status or lessons but does not constitute formal acceptance.

A sign-off is the formal acknowledgment and acceptance by the responsible stakeholders that deliverables or the project are approved. This mirrors BABOK v3, 5.5 Approve Requirements, where formal approval (sign-off) records stakeholder agreement and acceptance of deliverables.
Risk assessment analyzes uncertainty; it grants no acceptance or approval.
Inspection trend analysis is a quality analysis activity, not an acceptance mechanism.
The risk register contains identified risks and responses, not performance measurement analysis.
Work performance information is raw status data on work completed; it has not yet been evaluated into variance analysis and forecasts.

Performance reports take information from performance measurements and analyze it to provide variance analysis, earned value data, and forecasts, which feed risk monitoring and control. This is a project management (PMBOK-derived) concept consistent with the measurement and reporting ideas in BABOK v3, 10.28 Metrics and Key Performance Indicators, where measures are analyzed and reported to assess variance from expected performance.
The project management plan defines how the project is executed and controlled; it is not a source of measured performance data.
Assessment and performance are activities/outcomes, not the two categories of measurement data in BABOK.
Impact and dependencies are requirements attributes/traceability concepts, not evaluation data types.

When defining measures for evaluating solution performance, BABOK v3, 8.1 Measure Solution Performance, distinguishes quantitative measures (numerical, countable data) and qualitative measures (attitudes, perceptions, subjective judgments). End users evaluating solution effectiveness would rely on both data types.
Risk and value are separate analysis dimensions, not the two BABOK-defined types of performance measures.
Risk analysis and management identifies risks to stakeholder engagement, not their satisfaction with the relationship.
Brainstorming generates ideas; it is not aimed at assessing satisfaction with working relationships.
Collaborative games build shared understanding of a problem domain, not retrospective relationship improvement.

BABOK v3, 3.4 Manage Stakeholder Collaboration, lists Lessons Learned (10.27) as a technique used to understand stakeholders' satisfaction or dissatisfaction and to offer them an opportunity to help improve working relationships. This exactly matches the BA's goal of learning satisfaction levels and improving collaboration.
Backlogs manage prioritized work items, not sources of business rules.

Per BABOK v3, 10.9 Business Rules Analysis, business rules stem from and must be checked against sources such as organizational policies, in addition to regulations and contracts. Policies are the organizational directives from which many business rules are derived and constrained.
The business case justifies investment in the change; it is not a source of constraining rules.
A permissions matrix defines user access rights, not organizational business rules.

A RACI chart (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) is a chart-based representation of the roles and responsibilities of team members with respect to tasks or deliverables. In BABOK v3 the RACI matrix is described under 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas (and used in 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement) as a way to depict stakeholder roles and responsibilities for activities.
A Pareto chart displays frequency of causes/defects for prioritization, not roles.
A resource histogram shows resource utilization quantities over time, not responsibilities.
An organization chart shows reporting hierarchy and structure, not each member's responsibilities for tasks.
Face-to-face communication is characteristic of change-driven/adaptive approaches.
Chain of command describes organizational reporting, not a communication style prescribed by a plan-driven approach.

In BABOK, plan-driven (predictive) approaches rely on formal communication: pre-defined documents, official sign-offs, and written records of stakeholder interaction. BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach and 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement note that predictive approaches favor formal documentation and communication, whereas adaptive approaches favor frequent, informal channels.
Ad hoc communication is informal and unplanned, the opposite of a plan-driven approach.
An analysis task is simply a category of work, with no inherent sequencing constraint.

A dependent (successor) task is one that cannot start until one or more other tasks (its predecessors) finish. BABOK v3, 10.19 Estimation and 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach discuss sequencing tasks based on dependencies, where a dependent task relies on the completion of preceding work.
A predecessor task is the one that must complete first; it enables others rather than waiting on them.
An independent task by definition has no dependency on other tasks.

content: Define the risk and reward for the requirements
feedback: Per BABOK's description of modelling (BABOK v3, 4.2 Confirm Elicitation Results context and 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements), models describe and organize the domain: they categorize and create hierarchies, define boundaries of the business domain (matrices and diagrams), and depict business logic and processes. Defining the risk and reward of requirements is not a purpose of a model; risk is addressed through 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, not through modelling.

content: Categorize and create hierarchies of items
feedback: Categorizing items and creating hierarchies is a stated purpose of models (e.g., organizational and functional decomposition models).
Defining domain boundaries is a stated purpose of models (e.g., scope and context models).
Showing business logic is a stated purpose of models (e.g., decision tables, process and flow models).
Internal assets are one element of the current state (resources, tools), not the broad view of how value is delivered.
External influencers are outside forces (competition, regulators, customers), not the internal broad view analyzed here.
Change strategy is defined later (6.4) after current and future states are understood; it is not an evaluation of current value delivery.

BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State (.4 Elements) includes Business Architecture: the current state as a whole, showing how organizational structure, capabilities, processes, and technology fit together to deliver value to customers. Taking 'a broad view of the business... to understand how the company is currently providing value' is precisely an evaluation of the business architecture.
Risk estimation assesses probability and impact of already-identified risks.
Risk analysis evaluates identified risks; it does not start from discovering problems or sources.
Risk management is the overall umbrella process, not the specific step that begins with sources or problems.

Risk identification can proceed via source analysis (starting from the sources of risk) or problem analysis (starting from the problem/threat itself). This corresponds to the identification step within BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, where risks are first identified before being analyzed and treated.
Assumptions and constraints are outputs of requirements analysis activities, not Enterprise Analysis.
Stakeholder concerns are an elicitation result, not an Enterprise Analysis output.
Solution performance assessment is an output of Solution Assessment and Validation (v3: Solution Evaluation), not Enterprise Analysis.

In BABOK v2's Enterprise Analysis knowledge area, the five outputs are: business need, required capabilities, solution approach, solution scope, and business case. Solution approach is therefore an Enterprise Analysis output; in BABOK v3 this thinking maps to Strategy Analysis (6.4 Define Change Strategy).
Difficult stakeholder engagement argues against an adaptive approach, which depends on frequent stakeholder collaboration.

content: The risk of an incorrect implementation is unacceptably high.
feedback: Unacceptably high risk of incorrect implementation is a stated driver toward a predictive (plan-driven) approach.

content: Requirements can effectively be defined ahead of implementation.
feedback: Requirements definable ahead of implementation is a stated driver toward a predictive approach.

content: The organization's tolerance for uncertainty is high.
feedback: BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, states that adaptive approaches suit environments where the organization's (and stakeholders') tolerance for uncertainty is high and needs are discovered iteratively through feedback. Predictive approaches are favored when requirements can be defined up front and when the risk of incorrect implementation is unacceptably high.
Time tracking captures effort only; it does not measure quality, completeness, or stakeholder satisfaction.
Templates standardize deliverables but do not diagnose where the practice is underperforming.
Workload plans address capacity, not evidence of what is actually causing poor outcomes.

Because all evidence is anecdotal, the lead BA needs objective measures of business analysis performance to identify real improvement areas. BABOK v3 (performance analysis of business analysis work, supported by 10.28 Metrics and Key Performance Indicators) prescribes defining objective performance measures to objectively assess business analysis work and locate root causes of dissatisfaction.
A data-centric view focuses on information and its lifecycle, not on reusing manufacturing abilities.

The BA analyzed what the organization is able to do (manufacture vending machines) and proposed reusing that capability in a new product line. BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State and 10.6 Business Capability Analysis describe the capability-centric view, which is preferred when seeking innovative solutions that reuse existing organizational capabilities.
A process-centric view analyzes workflows for improvement; the proposal reuses a capability for a new product, not a process redesign.
A people-centric view focuses on roles, skills, and organizational structure, which is not the basis of this proposal.

content: The values, goals, and objectives that are relevant to the requirements
feedback: Relevant values, goals, and objectives are one of the required components of decision analysis.

content: The consequences of each possible decision
feedback: The consequences of each possible decision are a required component of decision analysis.

content: The nature of the decision that must be made
feedback: The nature of the decision to be made is a required component of decision analysis.

content: The future value of the requirements
feedback: Per BABOK's Decision Analysis technique (BABOK v3, 10.16 Decision Analysis; BABOK v2 9.8), effective decision analysis requires understanding the values/goals/objectives relevant to the decision, the nature of the decision, the areas of uncertainty, and the consequences of each possible decision. 'The future value of the requirements' is not one of these four components.
Updating is the act of revising the schedule, not the approved reference artifact.
Impacts are effects of changes, not a management-approved schedule product.

A baseline schedule is derived from the schedule model, aligned to the project execution plan, and approved by management to serve as the fixed reference against which progress and variances are measured. This baselining concept parallels BABOK v3 baselining of requirements in 5.4 Assess Requirements Changes / 5.1 Trace Requirements, where an approved baseline becomes the basis for comparison.
Dumping is not a schedule management concept at all.

content: It's important to identify the stakeholders so the business analyst knows who to report to.
feedback: Reporting lines matter but are not the primary business analysis reason for early stakeholder identification.

content: It's important to identify the stakeholders so the business analyst knows who to bill for the project.
feedback: Billing is a commercial/administrative concern, not a business analysis planning purpose.

content: It's important to identify the stakeholder so the business analyst can help ensure the timely delivery of the requirements deliverables.
feedback: Identifying stakeholders early (BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement; 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas) ensures the right people are involved in eliciting, reviewing, and approving requirements, which directly supports timely, complete delivery of requirements deliverables. Late discovery of stakeholders causes rework, missed requirements, and delays.

content: It's important to identify the stakeholders so the stakeholders know who the business analyst is.
feedback: Making the BA known to stakeholders is incidental; it does not address requirements quality or timeliness.
1200 results from an arithmetic error; 50x49/2 is 1225.

Communication channels are calculated as n(n-1)/2. With 50 stakeholders: 50 x 49 / 2 = 1,225 channels. This channel formula is standard communications-planning math consistent with stakeholder communication complexity considerations in BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement.
1500 does not correspond to n(n-1)/2 for n=50.
500 does not correspond to n(n-1)/2 for n=50.
'Time' is not part of SWOT; the T stands for Threats.
SWOT is not 'Stakeholder Weaknesses, Organizational Threats'.
SWOT is not 'Stakeholders, Weaknesses, Organization, Threats'.

Per BABOK v3, 10.46 SWOT Analysis, SWOT stands for Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats. It evaluates internal strengths and weaknesses against external opportunities and threats, often within Strategy Analysis.

content: The business case helps the organization determine if they can justify the investment for the solution.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 10.7 Business Cases, a business case provides the justification for a course of action by weighing expected benefits against costs, risks, and constraints, enabling the organization to decide whether the investment in the solution is justified. Justification of investment is its core purpose.

content: The business case helps the organization determine the duration and cost of the solution.
feedback: Duration and cost estimates may appear in a business case, but they are inputs to the justification, not its purpose.

content: The business case helps the organization determine the cost of the solution.
feedback: Cost alone is only one component; the purpose is comparing benefits to costs and risks.

content: The business case helps the organization determine if the solution is feasible.
feedback: Feasibility assessment informs the business case, but the case exists to justify the investment, not merely to prove feasibility.

content: The scribe must include the white board information as part of the minutes.
feedback: A scribe's minutes may not exist for all sessions and this is not a mandated rule for whiteboard use.

content: The business analyst must transfer the information to another medium before the whiteboard is erased.
feedback: Whiteboard content is a temporary record of elicitation results; BABOK's guidance on documenting elicitation outcomes (BABOK v3, 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results and 4.2 Conduct Elicitation) requires capturing the information in a persistent medium (photos, notes, models) before it is lost. The BA must transfer the whiteboard content to another medium before it is erased.

content: The business analyst must document the white board information and get all participants' approval of the recording.
feedback: Participant approval of the recording is part of confirming results, but the essential whiteboard-specific action is preserving the content first.

content: The regulator must approve the writings on the whiteboard.
feedback: No regulator approval applies to internal elicitation notes.
'Total ownership costing' is a distractor phrasing; the recognized term is total cost of ownership.
Lifecycle maintenance fees cover only maintenance, not the full post-implementation cost picture.
'Sustainability fees' is not a standard business analysis cost term.

Total cost of ownership (TCO) is the full cost of a solution over its life, including post-implementation operating, support, and maintenance costs charged after a vendor delivers it into production. BABOK v3 references TCO within financial analysis and business case evaluation (10.7 Business Cases, 10.20 Financial Analysis) as the measure of ongoing solution cost.

content: Communications in a change-driven approach to business analysis focus more on the frequency of communication.
feedback: The distinguishing characteristic is informal, in-person channels, not merely communication frequency.

content: Communications in a change-driven approach typically use face-to-face channels.
feedback: In a change-driven (adaptive) approach, communication is frequent and informal, favoring face-to-face channels over lengthy formal documentation, with details communicated in conversation and lightweight artifacts. This matches BABOK's contrast between approaches (BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach; BABOK v2 2.4), where change-driven approaches favor face-to-face communication and predictive approaches favor formal communication.

content: Communications in a change-driven approach focus more on formal communications.
feedback: Formal communications characterize plan-driven (predictive) approaches, not change-driven ones.

content: Communications in a change-driven approach are all ad hoc.
feedback: Communication is still planned and structured around iterations; it is not entirely ad hoc.

In the BABOK task for planning business analysis activities (Plan Business Analysis Approach / BABOK v2 2.3 Plan Business Analysis Activities), the inputs are the business analysis approach, business analysis performance assessment, organizational process assets, and the stakeholder list, roles, and responsibilities. A list of identified risks is not one of these inputs; risks are addressed through the Risk Analysis and Management technique (BABOK v3, 10.38), not as a planning input.
Organizational process assets are a defined input to planning business analysis activities.
The business analysis approach is a defined input that shapes how activities are planned.
The stakeholder list, roles, and responsibilities is a defined input used to determine which activities are needed.
Risk communication is only about sharing risk information with stakeholders, not setting tolerance or prioritizing.
Risk identification only discovers potential risks; it does not set tolerance or prioritize them.
Risk analysis assesses likelihood and impact of identified risks but does not set organizational risk tolerance.

Risk management is the overall, ongoing process that encompasses setting the organization's risk tolerance, identifying potential risks, analyzing and prioritizing them, and developing responses. Per BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, managing risks is a continuous activity that includes understanding stakeholder and enterprise risk tolerance and treating risks accordingly.

content: The organizational culture and type of information being approved
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance, element 'Plan for Approvals', when planning the approval process the business analyst considers the organizational culture and the type of information being approved, since these determine how formal the approvals must be and who must grant them.

content: The associated risks of foregoing or delaying an approval
feedback: Risks of foregoing or delaying an approval relate to the timing and frequency of approvals, not to defining the approval process itself.

content: The formality and rigor of the prioritization process
feedback: Formality and rigor of prioritization belong to planning the prioritization approach, a different element of governance planning.
Solution performance measures belong to Solution Evaluation, not to planning approvals.

The solution removes redundant maintenance of multiple routing variations and lets the business ship to new countries without IT change requests, which reduces effort, time, and rework. Per BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State (identifying opportunities) and 6.2 Define Future State, improvements that streamline work and remove bottlenecks are classified as increasing efficiencies.
Nothing in the scenario reduces the level of stakeholder involvement.
The opportunity is about streamlined routing operations, not improved access to information.
The benefit is operational flexibility after implementation, not a shorter project timeline.
The project life cycle is the entire series of phases a project passes through, not a single key event.
A phase is a grouping of project activities, not a single key event or deliverable marker.
'Achievement' is not a defined project management or BABOK term for progress measurement.

A milestone is a significant event or major deliverable in a project used to measure progress against the plan. BABOK v3 references milestones in Plan Business Analysis Activities and in Estimation (10.19) as checkpoints for tracking progress.
The future state may be an outcome of the discussion but does not define the boundaries that keep a workshop focused.
Assets are a factor in strategy analysis, not the key to keeping an elicitation session on topic.

Per BABOK v3, 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation and 4.2 Conduct Elicitation, the business analyst must understand the scope of the elicitation activity so the workshop stays on topic, the right stakeholders are engaged, and it can be recognized when the discussion strays outside the intended boundaries of the change.
The risk register tracks risks; it does not bound the topic of the workshop.
The business need is the fundamental input driving the business case.
Assumptions and constraints are a defined input used to qualify the business case estimates.

In the enterprise analysis task of defining the business case (BABOK v2 5.5), the inputs are the business need, solution scope, stakeholder concerns, and assumptions and constraints. Requirements verification is a Requirements Analysis quality activity, not an input to writing the business case; in BABOK v3 the business case is developed in Strategy Analysis (6.3/6.4) well before requirements are verified.
Stakeholder concerns are a defined input reflecting issues and risks to be addressed in the business case.
Outsourcing means having a third party perform the work or operate the process, not buying packaged software.
'Exploit' is not a solution approach; it is a risk-response strategy for positive risks.
'Create' means building a custom solution, the opposite of buying a COTS package.

Acquiring a commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) software package is a 'purchase' solution approach: the capability is bought from a vendor rather than built in-house. BABOK v3, 7.5 Define Design Options, discusses create vs. purchase (buy) vs. combinations as solution approaches for design options.

Per BABOK v3, 8.5 Recommend Actions to Increase Solution Value, 'do nothing' is the recommended action when the cost of a change is high relative to the expected benefit. The only improvement option costs ten times its benefits, while the current solution still delivers some value, so continuing with the implemented solution maximizes net value.
Retiring the solution would destroy the value the business is already receiving without a justified replacement.
Conducting elicitation does not address the decision; the options and their costs/benefits are already known.
Planning stakeholder engagement is a Business Analysis Planning task and does not resolve the value question.

In stakeholder analysis (BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Stakeholder Engagement; BABOK v2 2.2 Conduct Stakeholder Analysis), the survey/questionnaire technique (BABOK v3, 10.45) is identified as useful for identifying shared characteristics of a stakeholder group because it can collect uniform information from many stakeholders at once.
Scope modeling is used to identify stakeholders who interact with the solution boundary, not their shared characteristics.
Brainstorming is used to produce the initial list of stakeholders and roles, not to profile shared characteristics.
Interviews gather individual perspectives one at a time rather than characteristics shared across a group.
