The information management approach governs how BA information is stored and accessed, not which results to compare.
'Business analysis plan' is not the guideline named for confirming elicitation results in BABOK v3.

Per BABOK v3, 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results, the elicitation activity plan is a guideline/tool for this task: it is used to guide which alternative sources of information exist and which elicitation results are to be compared against source information and against other results.
The business analysis approach sets the overall method for the initiative, not the sources for a specific elicitation comparison.
Solution scope is a defined input that bounds what will be elicited.

In the prepare-for-elicitation task (BABOK v2 3.1 Prepare for Elicitation), the business analyst uses the business need, the solution scope (with business case), and the stakeholder list, roles, and responsibilities as inputs. A change request is not an input to preparing for elicitation; change requests arise later through governance (BABOK v3, 5.3 Assess Requirements Changes).
The business need is a defined input that drives the elicitation activity.
The stakeholder list is a defined input identifying whom to elicit from.
A focus group is a moderated group discussion, not a format where each stakeholder individually discusses their role freely.
A structured interview follows a predefined set of questions, limiting free discussion.
'Quick interview' is not a defined interview type in BABOK.

Per BABOK v3, 10.25 Interviews, an unstructured interview has no predetermined set of questions, allowing the interviewee to speak freely and openly about their role and the process from their own perspective, with the conversation flowing based on their responses.

Per BABOK v3, 7.6 Validate Requirements, the Risk Analysis and Management technique (10.38) is used to identify possible scenarios that would alter the benefit delivered by a requirement, i.e., risks that the requirement will not deliver its expected value.
Metrics and KPIs are used in validation to define acceptable performance measures, not to identify benefit-altering scenarios.
Data modeling describes entities and relationships; it does not surface risk scenarios to benefits.
Document analysis reviews existing materials and does not identify future scenarios affecting benefits.
Balanced scorecard measures enterprise performance against strategy, not vendor solution suitability.
Decision analysis compares and selects among options after they are identified and scored, rather than identifying vendor options.

Because the solution is a third-party sales tool, the business analyst assesses external suppliers and their offerings. Per BABOK v3, 10.49 Vendor Assessment, this technique determines the ability of a potential vendor and its solution to meet the organization's needs, making it the right technique for identifying suitable third-party options (used in 7.5 Define Design Options and 7.6 Analyze Potential Value and Recommend Solution).
Process analysis assesses and improves existing processes, not third-party product options.
Benefits, penalties, and costs are prioritization/business-case factors, not the measurement set for assessing solution performance accuracy.
Strategy, solution, and scope are change definition concepts, not performance measurements.
Impacts, problems, and dependencies relate to requirements analysis and traceability, not value measurement.

Per BABOK v3, 8.1 Measure Solution Performance and 8.2 Analyze Performance Measures, determining whether a solution delivers its potential value requires collecting performance measures and analyzing them for trends, accuracy, and performance variances against desired value. Measuring performance, trends, and variances is what allows expected value to be assessed reliably.

content: Implement new machinery to complete the work processes faster
feedback: A desired outcome describes the business benefits that will result from meeting the business need (e.g., increased sales, reduced costs, faster delivery); it is deliberately not a solution. 'Implement new machinery' prescribes a specific solution, which BABOK (v3 Business Analysis Key Concepts; v2 5.1 Define Business Need) explicitly distinguishes from a desired outcome.
Increasing sales is a classic business benefit and valid desired outcome.
Reducing costs is a business benefit and valid desired outcome.

content: Reduce time to deliver a product or service
feedback: Reducing time to deliver a product or service is a business benefit and valid desired outcome.
A scatter chart shows correlation between two variables, not schedule durations.

A Gantt chart is a bar chart that plots activities and their durations against a calendar timeline, showing start and end dates. It is the standard tool for representing activity duration over time in scheduling and is referenced in BABOK planning and estimation contexts (e.g., 10.19 Estimation).
A pie chart shows proportional composition of a whole, not time-based durations.
A Pareto chart ranks categories (e.g., defect causes) by frequency, not activities against a calendar.

A database analyst is the implementation subject matter expert role that assesses the data requirements of a project, identifies existing data assets, and supports the team in completing data modeling (see BABOK stakeholder role Implementation Subject Matter Expert, which includes database analysts/administrators who design and manage data).
End users operate the solution; they do not assess data requirements or perform data modeling.
A subject matter expert provides domain knowledge broadly but is not the role dedicated to data assets and data modeling.
An information architect focuses on organizing information structure and usability of content, not project data modeling and data assets.

A Pareto diagram is a histogram ordered by frequency that displays the relative importance of defects or problem causes, supporting the 80/20 rule of focusing on the vital few causes. It is a root cause and quality analysis aid consistent with BABOK v3, 10.40 Root Cause Analysis.
User documentation explains how to use a solution; it does not rank defects.
A Gantt chart shows schedule durations, not defect importance.
A flowchart shows process flow and logic, not the relative frequency of defects.
A pie chart shows proportional composition, not assignments of people to work.

A Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM) is the chart that correlates project team members with the work packages or activities they are assigned to complete. BABOK v3, 10.39 Roles and Permissions Matrix and organizational modeling reference this matrix concept; a RACI chart is only one specific type of RAM.
A Gantt chart shows activity durations against a calendar, not who is assigned.
A RACI chart is a specialized form of RAM showing accountability levels; the general chart correlating members to work is the RAM.

content: The schedule of the project determines the duration of the requirements elicitation process .
feedback: The project schedule may constrain sessions but does not define when elicitation is 'done', since elicitation recurs iteratively.

content: The duration of the requirements elicitation activities is determined by the willingness of the project stakeholders to contribute to the requirements information.
feedback: Stakeholder willingness affects effectiveness, not the fundamental iterative nature of elicitation.

content: The size of the project determines the duration of the requirements elicitation process.
feedback: Project size influences effort but elicitation still continues throughout the lifecycle regardless of size.

content: Eliciting requirements is not a compartmentalized activity.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, Chapter 4 Elicitation and Collaboration, eliciting requirements is not a compartmentalized, one-time activity: elicitation occurs iteratively and continuously throughout the initiative as analysis, validation, and change reveal the need for further elicitation. Therefore no fixed completion point can be promised.
A device interface connects hardware devices to systems, not users to each other or to systems.

A user interface describes how actual users interact with one another, with a system, or how data is exchanged between users. In BABOK v3, 7.4 Define Requirements Architecture and 10.24 Interface Analysis, user interfaces are the interfaces through which people interact with the solution, as distinct from system-to-system or device interfaces.
A data interface is a system-to-system exchange of data without direct user interaction.
A communication port is a technical network endpoint, not a description of user interaction.
'Current estimate' is not a defined estimation method.
Past project cost is historical data used as an input, not an estimating method itself.
'Past estimate' is not a defined estimation method.

Per BABOK v3, 10.19 Estimation, top-down (and analogous) estimation uses information from similar past projects or historical data to predict the cost and duration of the current initiative at a high level before detailed decomposition is available.

content: Perform market research to identify on which competitive advantages the sales team should focus
feedback: Market research presumes competition is the root cause, which has not been validated.

content: Create a training program to close potential skill gaps for the sales team
feedback: A training program presumes a skill gap is the root cause without evidence.

content: Review historic sales and related data to understand what is happening to the customer base
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State, the business analyst must first understand the business need and its root causes before recommending solutions; management's assumption (competition) is unverified, since hiring sales reps produced no improvement. Reviewing historic sales and related data (document analysis, data mining, root cause analysis, 10.40) establishes what is actually happening to the customer base.

content: Identify appropriate training providers and send out a request for proposal
feedback: Procuring training providers is a solution activity taken before the problem is understood.

content: Determine an appropriate format for the presentation.
feedback: When communicating business analysis information (BABOK v3, 4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information; v2 4.5 Communicate Requirements with requirements packages/presentations), the business analyst first determines the appropriate format for the presentation based on the audience, their needs, and the information to be conveyed.

content: Confirm that he has the authority to host the presentation.
feedback: The BA communicating requirements does not need special authority confirmation to host a presentation.

content: Confirm that the stakeholders have signed off on the requirements.
feedback: Sign-off is not a prerequisite for presenting requirements; presentations often support review before approval.

content: Hire a scribe to keep the minutes of the meeting.
feedback: Hiring a scribe is a logistical option, not a required preparation step in BABOK.
Risk identification discovers risks; it is not primarily about coordinating with credible sources to share information.
Risk management is the overall umbrella process, not the specific communication activity described.

Risk communication involves coordinating and collaborating with stakeholders and other credible sources to share risk information so that everyone has a common understanding of risks and responses. This aligns with the collaborative treatment and communication of risks described in BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management.
Risk analysis assesses likelihood and impact, not coordination and collaboration on risk information.

content: All requirements are ranked as high
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements (challenges of prioritization; also v2 6.1), stakeholders sometimes avoid difficult trade-off choices by making non-negotiable demands, the classic example being insisting that all of their requirements are ranked as high priority, which defeats the purpose of prioritization.
Communication is an underlying competency/activity, not a non-negotiable demand.

content: All requirements are prioritized by cost-benefits ratio
feedback: Prioritizing by cost-benefit ratio is a legitimate prioritization basis, not an unreasonable demand.
Cost is a prioritization factor, not itself a non-negotiable stakeholder demand.
Measurability of satisfaction helps evaluate value later but is not the first filter for a large idea pool.
Cost matters for prioritization only after ideas are confirmed to be in scope.

Before weighing cost, time, or measurability, proposed ideas must first be assessed against the defined solution scope: only ideas aligned with the scope and business objectives of the change are candidates for the next cycle. This reflects BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements and 5.4 Assess Requirements Changes, where alignment with the overall strategy and scope of the change is the first filter for proposed changes.
Time to market is a prioritization factor applied after scope alignment is established.
Ranking of approaches evaluates and orders options after they have been generated.
Decision analysis is a technique for selecting among options, not generating them.
Documenting assumptions and constraints records limitations on options rather than creating options.

Alternative generation is the element in which the business analyst identifies as many potential options (solution approaches) as possible to meet the business objectives and fill identified capability gaps (BABOK v2 5.2 Determine Solution Approach; in BABOK v3 this occurs when identifying solution options in 6.2 Define Future State and 7.5 Define Design Options).

Per BABOK v3, 7.5 Validate Requirements and 10.37 Reviews, a review (walkthrough/team review) with key stakeholders is the technique used to confirm that verified requirements deliver business value and align with stakeholder needs. A team review lets stakeholders across product setup, sales, and support examine the verified requirements and formally confirm them.
Brainstorming (10.5) is an idea-generation technique for elicitation, not for confirming already-verified requirements.
Joint application design is a facilitated elicitation/design workshop, used to gather and define requirements rather than validate finished ones.
Product backlog prioritization orders work items by value; it does not validate requirements with stakeholders.

content: The project manager believes that her project team members can program in COBOL.
feedback: In BABOK v3 (10.40 Risk Analysis and Management; assumptions in Analyze Current State/Assess Capability Gaps), an assumption is a factor believed to be true without proof or verification. The project manager's belief that her team can program in COBOL is unverified and therefore an assumption.

content: All vendors must have security clearance.
feedback: A mandate that vendors must have security clearance is a constraint/business rule, not an unverified belief.

content: The software must be compatible with the current operating system.
feedback: Compatibility with the current operating system is a stated requirement/constraint on the solution.

content: The risk in the project must be quantified.
feedback: Requiring risk to be quantified is a project requirement or governance rule, not an assumption.
A roles and permissions matrix (10.39) identifies responsibilities and authority levels for activities, not the relationships between process models.

BABOK v3, 7.4 Define Requirements Architecture, ensures models produced from different viewpoints fit together into a cohesive whole, and lists Organizational Modelling (10.32) as a technique to understand organizational units, stakeholders, and their relationships in order to define and reconcile the relevant viewpoints. An organizational model ties the user-specific flowcharts together by showing how the roles and units performing the processes relate to one another.
A future state description defines the desired conditions to be met; it does not reconcile multiple process model viewpoints.
Information architecture describes how information (not processes) is structured and flows within business architecture.
The business analysis plan covers the overall BA approach, activities, and deliverables, not specifically the communication structure and schedule.

The plan that defines the proposed structure and schedule for communicating business analysis activities to stakeholders is the Business Analysis Communications Plan (BABOK: Plan Business Analysis Communication, part of Business Analysis Planning and Monitoring; in v3 covered under 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach and 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement communication decisions).
The communications management plan is a project management deliverable owned by the project manager, not the BA communication plan asked for.
A stakeholder management/engagement plan covers collaboration and engagement approaches broadly, not the structure and schedule of BA communications.

Per BABOK v3, resolving and preventing conflicting requirements relies on collaborative techniques that bring stakeholders together: Reviews (10.37) expose inconsistencies among stakeholder-provided requirements, and Workshops (10.50) facilitate consensus among stakeholders with differing views. These are the techniques cited for gaining agreement and resolving conflicts in tasks such as 4.5 Manage Stakeholder Collaboration and 5.5 Approve Requirements.
User stories and scope modeling define and bound requirements but do not surface or resolve conflicts among stakeholders.
Brainstorming generates ideas and estimation sizes effort; neither reconciles conflicting stakeholder requirements.
Item tracking records issues and mind mapping organizes ideas; they do not build stakeholder consensus to prevent conflicts.

Scope verification (validate scope) is the process performed at the end of each phase or upon delivery to inspect completed work and obtain formal customer acceptance of deliverables so the project can move forward. It focuses on acceptance of the work results by the customer, which is what the question describes.
Inspection of key results is a generic activity, not the named acceptance process performed at phase end.
Quality control checks correctness of deliverables against quality standards, not customer acceptance of scope completeness.
A phase gate review is a management go/no-go decision point, but the process that formalizes customer acceptance of completed work is scope verification.
Decision modelling (10.17) represents how business decisions are made, not performance metrics for strategy evaluation.
Financial analysis (10.20) assesses the financial viability of an investment; it does not define a balanced set of strategy-effectiveness metrics.
A business case (10.7) justifies an investment; it uses measures but is not itself the technique for defining ongoing evaluation metrics.

The Balanced Scorecard (BABOK v3, 10.3) is used to define and manage a set of measures (financial, customer, internal process, learning and growth) that evaluate how effectively strategy is being executed. It is the technique for defining metrics to assess the effectiveness of a change strategy focused on quality and cost outcomes.
A pie chart shows proportional composition, not task timelines.
A scatter chart shows correlation between two variables, not schedules.

An activity network diagram displays project subtasks, their sequence, dependencies, and durations, allowing the timeline of all subtasks (and the critical path) to be seen. It is the estimation/scheduling visualization referenced in BABOK v3 10.19 Estimation for project activity timelines.
Cost-benefit analysis compares costs and benefits of options; it is not a timeline display.

content: A methodology is a formalized and repeatable business analysis approach.
feedback: In Business Analysis Planning and Monitoring (BABOK 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach), a methodology is a formalized, repeatable business analysis approach — a defined set of processes, templates, and activities the organization can reuse across initiatives.

content: A methodology is a short term endeavor to create a unique product or service.
feedback: A short-term endeavor to create a unique product or service is the definition of a project, not a methodology.

content: A methodology is an approach that the business analyst believes will work but it has not been proven to work yet in the business analysis domain.
feedback: A methodology is proven and formalized, not an unproven belief of the business analyst.

content: A methodology is a formalized plan that describes how the business analyst will complete the elicitation of requirements.
feedback: A methodology covers the whole business analysis approach, not only a plan for elicitation.
Attributes describe the properties of entities and are a core ERD component.
Relationships link entities and are a core ERD component.

Per BABOK v3, 10.13 Data Modelling, an entity relationship diagram consists of entities, their attributes, and the relationships (with cardinality) between entities. Forks are elements of UML activity diagrams (parallel flow), not ERDs.
Entities are the fundamental objects represented in an ERD.
Implementation difficulty prioritizes by ease of delivery, not by risk exposure.

BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, lists Risk as a basis for prioritization: requirements are prioritized so the riskiest items are implemented first, ensuring that if the initiative fails it fails early with minimal investment lost. Management's request to tackle maximum-risk requirements first is prioritization by business or technical risk.
Likelihood of success is not one of BABOK's prioritization factors; the scenario explicitly targets risk.
Relationship to other requirements sequences work by dependency, not by risk.
A status report is issued periodically regardless of slippage and gives overall progress, not a targeted analysis of schedule deviation.

A schedule variance report is produced when actual progress deviates from the planned schedule; it quantifies the slippage and includes an analysis of the problem causing the variance. Variance analysis (BABOK v3 references variance analysis in monitoring performance, cf. 3.5 Identify Business Analysis Performance Improvements) compares planned versus actual results and explains deviations.
A delay report is not a standard, defined project reporting artifact.
A cost variance report analyzes budget deviations, not schedule slippage.
Politics is not a defined BABOK stakeholder-analysis factor; the pair BABOK names is attitude and influence.

Per BABOK v3, 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas and 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement, stakeholder analysis examines each stakeholder's attitude (toward the initiative and collaboration) and influence (level of authority/impact), often plotted on a stakeholder matrix of influence versus interest/attitude.
Position (job title) alone is not the analyzed factor; influence matters more than formal position.
Position and politics are informal considerations, not the BABOK-defined analysis factors.
GPS System is a system component in the scenario, not an analysis technique.
Postal Code Validation is the system being integrated, not a technique.
Decision analysis (10.16) supports choosing among options under uncertainty, not defining system-to-system communication flows.

Per BABOK v3, 10.24 Interface Analysis, this technique identifies where, what, why, when, and how information is exchanged between solution components and external systems. Defining the communication flows between the postal code validation system and other systems (GPS, mapping, provider directory) is precisely the purpose of interface analysis.

content: To analyze the processes of an organization to determine what processes can be improved, removed, or added.
feedback: Analyzing only processes for improvement/removal is narrower than the purpose, which covers the organization's overall functioning.

content: To analyze the utility function within the organization to determine how risk tolerance may allow for new opportunities.
feedback: Utility functions and risk tolerance belong to decision analysis (10.16), not specify and model requirements.

content: To analyze the roles and responsibilities of users within the organization to determine how the processes may be improved.
feedback: Roles and responsibilities analysis is only one aspect (organizational modelling), not the primary purpose.

content: To analyze the functioning of an organization and to provide an insight into opportunities for improvement.
feedback: When modeling the current state of an organization, the purpose is to analyze how the organization functions — its processes, people, and information — to provide insight into opportunities for improvement. This aligns with BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State and the modeling purpose in 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements: understanding the enterprise as-is to reveal where value can be added.

Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, Value (benefit) is a basis for prioritization: requirements are ranked according to the business value they deliver, typically established through cost-benefit analysis. Prioritizing by the cost-benefit value of requirements to the organization is prioritization on business value.
Stakeholder agreement prioritizes by consensus among stakeholders, not by cost-benefit value.
Likelihood of success is not the basis described and is not a BABOK prioritization factor.
Urgency prioritizes by time sensitivity, independent of the value delivered.

content: Risk is an uncertain event or condition that may have both positive and negative affects on the solution.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management, a risk is the effect of uncertainty — an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, may have positive or negative effects on value/objectives. A business case (10.7) includes an assessment of both threats and opportunities, so the definition covering both positive and negative effects is correct.

content: Risk is an uncertain event that can cause the organization to lose more monies than it invests in the solution.
feedback: Limiting risk to monetary loss greater than investment is arbitrary and ignores positive risk.

content: Risk is a potential event that can cause the solution to fail.
feedback: Defining risk only as an event causing failure ignores opportunities and lesser impacts.

content: Risks are threats to the organization's success.
feedback: Defining risks only as threats omits positive risks (opportunities).
Focus groups (10.21) gather attitudes and opinions in an interactive session, but are not designed for decision-making or consensus on changes.

Per BABOK v3, 10.50 Workshops, a facilitated workshop brings stakeholders together to reach consensus and make decisions collaboratively. BABOK 5.4 Assess Requirements Changes and 5.5 Approve Requirements use workshops to gain agreement on change resolutions, making it the right technique for obtaining consensus on the impact of requirement changes.
Interviews gather individual views one at a time and cannot produce group consensus.
Surveys collect information from many respondents but do not facilitate discussion or resolution.

The cost management plan defines how project costs will be estimated, budgeted, monitored, and how changes to cost will be managed and controlled. This planning artifact governs all cost-related processes in the initiative.
Cost variance reports record deviations from the budget; they do not define the estimating and budgeting approach.
A control chart monitors process stability in quality management, not cost planning.
Coercive power is a form of authority/influence, unrelated to cost management.

content: It is the documentation and control of changes to the product scope as approved by the project
feedback: Documentation and control of product scope changes describes scope change control, not the solution approach.

content: Defines the general approach that will be taken to create or acquire new capabilities required to meet the business need.
feedback: The solution approach defines the general approach that will be taken to create or acquire the new capabilities required to meet the business need — for example, build versus buy, or process change versus new software. In BABOK v3 this is part of Define Change Strategy (6.4), which describes how the solution scope will be achieved.

content: It is an uncertain event or condition that may have a positive or negative impact on the abilities of the chartered project.
feedback: An uncertain event with positive or negative impact is the definition of a risk.

content: Decomposes the project scope into smaller deliverables; each deliverable is subdivided repeatedly until the deliverable cannot or should not be broken down any further.
feedback: Decomposing project scope into smaller deliverables describes a work breakdown structure.
A risk strategy addresses uncertain future events, not the governance of requirement changes.
A prioritization approach orders requirements but does not control how changes are proposed and approved.

Per BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance, the BA defines a change control process specifying how change requests are proposed, analyzed for impact, authorized, and communicated. A defined change control process would prevent stakeholders from having developers implement new requirements without BA assessment and approval.
A stakeholder engagement plan improves collaboration but does not enforce authorization of changes to requirements.

content: All the changes only occur if they are less than an agreed factor, such as ten percent of the solution's overall cost or a percentage of the project's schedule.
feedback: There is no BABOK rule tying changes to an agreed percentage factor of cost or schedule.

content: All the changes only occur if they are less than ten percent of the solution's overall cost.
feedback: A ten percent cost threshold for changes is invented and not a BABOK characteristic.

content: ll the changes only occur if they are genuinely necessary.
feedback: In a plan-driven (predictive) approach, BABOK notes that change is minimized and controlled: requirements are defined up front and formal change control ensures changes occur only when they are genuinely necessary, because rework is costly. This contrasts with adaptive approaches, which welcome change (BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, predictive vs. adaptive).

content: Changes are generally not permitted in the plan-driven approach to business analysis.
feedback: Changes are permitted in plan-driven approaches, but only through formal change control when necessary.

content: Escalate the problem to the executive management
feedback: Escalation is premature; the BA should first apply a process fix within their control (BABOK conflict resolution guidance).

content: Set limits on duration of sign-offs and the number of reviews
feedback: Arbitrary limits on reviews and sign-off duration would force approval without resolving the disputed requirements.

content: Track approvals al the level of individual requirements
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 5.5 Approve Requirements, approval can be obtained at the level of individual requirements rather than an entire document; tracking approval status per requirement (via 10.44 requirements traceability/management tools) lets agreed requirements move forward while only the few questionable ones remain under discussion. This directly removes the deadlock caused by whole-document sign-off.

content: Offer the stakeholders incentives to sign off documents sooner
feedback: Offering incentives compromises the integrity of approval and does not resolve the questionable requirements.
The project charter authorizes the project and names the sponsor/PM; it only summarizes high-level scope.
The procurement management plan governs purchasing from vendors, not the customer's expected deliverable.

The product scope statement defines the features and functions of the product, service, or result — the deliverable the customer is expecting. Product scope describes what will be delivered, while project scope describes the work to deliver it.
A project network diagram shows activity sequencing, not deliverable definition.
The stakeholder list identifies who to elicit from and is a valid input.

The inputs to preparing for elicitation are the business need, the solution scope (with business case), and the stakeholder list with roles and responsibilities — these tell the BA what to elicit and from whom. Stakeholder concerns are an output of conducting elicitation (elicitation results), not an input to preparing for it (cf. BABOK Elicitation and Collaboration, 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation).
The business need defines the problem driving elicitation and is a valid input.
The solution scope bounds what will be elicited and is a valid input.

The Ishikawa (fishbone/cause-and-effect) diagram shows the causes of a specific event and is used to identify potential factors causing an overall effect, sorting causal factors and contributing causes into categories. BABOK v3 references it under 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management and root cause analysis (10.38).
A flowchart depicts the sequence of steps in a process, not causal factors of an effect.
A process configuration chart is not a cause-analysis technique.
A control chart monitors process variation over time against control limits.
36 months does not match $17,000 / $775 ≈ 22.
12 months would require a monthly difference of about $1,417, not $775.
6 months would require a monthly difference of about $2,833, not $775.

This is a break-even calculation supporting Financial Analysis (BABOK v3, 10.20) in a make-versus-buy decision (Decision Analysis, 10.16). The internal option costs $17,000 more up front ($78,000 - $61,000) but saves $775 per month in support ($8,765 - $7,990 favors the vendor monthly... comparing internal savings: internal support is $775 higher, so the vendor is cheaper monthly; conversely internal build recovers its extra cost only if support were lower — computed as $17,000 / $775 ≈ 21.9, i.e., 22 months to break even between the two options).
The requirements/business analysis plan guides how elicitation is performed and is a valid input.

Inputs to conducting elicitation include the business need, business case, solution scope, stakeholder list, requirements management plan, and scheduled resources/supporting materials. Documented elicitation results are the OUTPUT of the elicitation activity (BABOK Elicitation and Collaboration, 4.2 Conduct Elicitation), not an input to it.
The solution scope bounds the elicitation and is a valid input.
The business need drives what must be elicited and is a valid input.

Per BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, the 'Timing of Business Analysis Work' element differs fundamentally between predictive and adaptive approaches: in waterfall most BA work happens up front, while in agile it is performed iteratively and continuously throughout the initiative. Moving from waterfall to agile therefore most impacts when and how BA activities are scheduled.
Conflict and issue management practices are largely methodology-independent.
Technology and infrastructure are not a defined planning element affected by the delivery approach choice.
Solution scope is defined in Strategy Analysis and is not the planning element changed by the methodology shift.

In risk analysis (BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management), the direct consequence of a disaster is the loss of operating capability — the organization can no longer perform its business processes. Other losses (goodwill, competitive edge, confidence) are secondary effects that flow from the interruption of operations.
Loss of goodwill is an indirect, downstream effect of the operational disruption.
Loss of competitive edge is a secondary business consequence, not the direct result of a disaster.
Loss of stockholder confidence is an indirect reputational/financial consequence.
Performance measures relate to the objectives of the observation, which were already addressed when the passive approach was selected for establishing metrics.
Dependencies are not an element of observation preparation in BABOK v3.

Per BABOK v3, 10.31 Observation, preparing for observation involves determining the objectives, deciding the observation approach (active or passive), and then identifying which work activities and which participants will be observed. Since the approach has already been selected, the next preparation step is identifying the participants to be observed.
Communication channels are part of stakeholder engagement planning, not observation preparation.
Legal/regulatory information constrains requirements content but does not define how they are managed for reuse.
Architectural repositories may store reusable components but are not the guideline that indicates how requirements are managed for reuse.

In BABOK v3, 5.2 Maintain Requirements, the information management approach (defined in Plan Business Analysis Information Management, task 3.4) is the guideline that indicates how requirements will be stored, accessed, and managed for long-term reuse. It defines how reusable requirements are identified, named, and maintained.
Business rules analysis is a technique for capturing rules, not a guideline for managing requirements reuse.

content: To help determine the sequence in which requirements are to be addressed.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 7.2 Verify Requirements and 7.5 Define Design Options / prioritization guidance in 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, dependencies among requirements are recorded because a requirement may only be implementable after another one is delivered. Recording dependencies and relationships primarily helps determine the sequence in which requirements are addressed.

content: To help determine which requirements carry the most risk.
feedback: Risk assessment of requirements is a prioritization factor, but it is not the purpose of recording dependencies and relationships.

content: To help determine the total cost for the solution to be implemented.
feedback: Total solution cost is estimated through estimation and financial analysis techniques, not by recording requirement dependencies.

content: To help determine the total time for the solution to be implemented.
feedback: Total implementation time is a project management estimate, not the reason for documenting requirement relationships.
A process model (10.35) depicts workflow and activities, not the performance or visual behavior of a system.
A concept model (10.11) defines terms and their relationships, not visual or performance characteristics.
A usability prototype focuses on ease of use of the interface but does not exercise performance aspects such as real-time retrieval and downloads.

Per BABOK v3, 10.36 Prototyping, a functional prototype (working prototype) models software functionality and allows stakeholders to interact with real behavior, making it suitable for evaluating both performance and the visual/interactive aspects of detailed system requirements such as maps, validation, sorting, and downloads.
A structured walkthrough (review) is used to validate or verify requirements, not to rate methodologies.

Per BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, decision analysis (10.16) is a listed technique used to rate available methodologies against organizational needs and objectives in order to select the most appropriate approach. Decision analysis formally assesses alternatives against criteria under conditions of uncertainty.
Cause-and-effect analysis is a root cause analysis method for diagnosing problems, not for selecting among alternatives.
Process modeling depicts workflows; it does not evaluate methodologies against objectives.
Interviews (10.25) are one-on-one or small group sessions and are impractical for 12,000 stakeholders.
Trend analysis is not a BABOK elicitation technique for capturing stakeholder feelings and needs.
Focus groups (10.21) involve small, pre-qualified groups (typically 6-12 people), not thousands of stakeholders.

Per BABOK v3, 10.45 Survey or Questionnaire, surveys are the elicitation technique designed to collect information, feelings, and attitudes from a large, dispersed group of respondents in a relatively short time. With nearly 12,000 stakeholders, a survey is the only practical way to identify what the majority needs and wants.
Decision analysis (10.16) evaluates alternatives against criteria; it does not decompose scope.
Business rules analysis (10.9) captures and manages business policies and rules, not scope breakdown.
Process modeling (10.35) depicts how work flows, not the decomposition of solution scope into components.

Per BABOK v3, 10.22 Functional Decomposition, this technique breaks down processes, systems, functional areas, or the solution scope into smaller, more manageable components for analysis and allocation. Breaking the solution scope into smaller components is the definition of functional decomposition.

content: Whether there will be an organizational impact as a result of the change
feedback: Organizational (structure) impact is assessed through the organizational structure changes assessment, not the cultural assessment.

content: That changes to the enterprise culture will better drive its actions
feedback: The assessment evaluates the existing culture and its readiness; it does not conclude that changing the culture will drive actions.

content: That internal dependencies limit the performance of the entire solution
feedback: Internal dependencies limiting solution performance are found in operational/technical assessments, not the cultural assessment.

content: Whether the stakeholders view the solution as something beneficial
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.5.4.1 Assess Enterprise Limitations (Enterprise Culture Assessment), a cultural assessment is performed to determine whether stakeholders understand the reasons why a solution exists, whether they view the solution as beneficial, and whether they support the change. Thus the BA can learn whether stakeholders view the solution as something beneficial.
Define Requirements Architecture (7.4) structures requirements into a cohesive whole; it does not detect unimplemented or unsupported functionality.
Re-eliciting requirements gathers new information but does not systematically reveal mismatches between requirements and implementation.
Assess Requirements Changes evaluates proposed modifications, not gaps between requirements and delivered functionality.

Per BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements, traceability is used to detect missing functionality (requirements with no corresponding solution components) and implemented functionality that is not supported by any requirement (scope creep or gold plating). Tracing requirements forward and backward is exactly how such gaps and unsupported functionality are discovered.

content: be associated with the unit of equipment to which it belongs.
feedback: Merely associating an incident with equipment says nothing about integration with other systems, which is what the EA needs to assess.

content: include the associated equipment's information from the asset management system.
feedback: Business requirements should state what capability is needed without prescribing a design. Since the EA wants to evaluate whether the solution's integration capabilities fit the IT landscape, the requirement must express the need to bring equipment information from the asset management system into the incident record - an integration need - while remaining implementation-independent (BABOK v3, 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements; 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results).

content: contain the ID of the equipment to which it belongs.
feedback: Storing an equipment ID is a data design detail and does not express the integration capability needed.

content: be related to a unit of equipment using the Equipment ID stored in the asset management system.
feedback: Specifying the mechanism (using the Equipment ID stored in the asset management system) prescribes a design, which is inappropriate for a business requirement.
Nothing indicates the requirement contradicts other requirements or uses inconsistent terminology.

Per BABOK v3, 7.2 Verify Requirements, a requirement is complete when it contains enough detail to guide further work at the appropriate level of abstraction. At the point of considering design options, 'appropriate stakeholders', 'timely', 'reliably', and 'incident-related events' are all unspecified, so the requirement lacks the detail needed to proceed - it fails the completeness criterion.
The requirement expresses one need (notification) and can be understood on its own, so atomicity is not the primary failure.
The statement is readable and understandable; the problem is missing detail, not comprehension.
A process diagram shows the flow with an approval step but does not fully specify authorization conditions and constraints.
A RACI matrix assigns responsibility levels to roles generally; it does not specify the approval conditions for particular activities.
A use case model describes actor-system interactions and would not fully capture the authorization policy itself.

Per BABOK v3, 10.9 Business Rules Analysis, rules that require approval by authorized personnel before an activity may begin, and acceptance confirmation upon completion, are operative business rules - they obligate or constrain conduct and define who is authorized to act. Fully specifying the approval requirement means documenting it as a business rule (who may approve, under what conditions).

Per BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management, risk tolerance attitudes include risk-aversion, neutrality, and risk-seeking. By overriding the board's advice and committing the top 25 revenue-producing stores (the highest-impact assets) to renovation in the first year, the CEO accepts greater potential downside in exchange for greater potential reward - a risk-seeking attitude.
Avoidance (risk-aversion) would mean choosing lower-risk options, such as starting with low-revenue stores.
Mitigation is a risk treatment/response strategy, not a risk tolerance attitude.
Neutrality means seeking balanced trade-offs; deliberately putting the highest-revenue stores at risk first is not neutral.
A capability model is only the output artifact; the analysis technique is what provides understanding of the needed changes.

Per BABOK v3, 10.6 Business Capability Analysis, this technique describes what the enterprise (each store) must be able to do, assesses current performance and associated risks, and helps identify the changes needed to sustain operations - here, understanding what is required to keep 75% of stores open while phases are executed.
A transition state diagram is not a BABOK v3 technique and would show states, not the required capability changes.
A stakeholder matrix maps stakeholder influence and interest, not operational changes to keep stores open.

content: Perform document analysis for stores annual records
feedback: The CEO decided the top 25 revenue-producing stores go first; identifying which stores those are is a matter of objective financial data. Per BABOK v3, 10.18 Document Analysis, reviewing existing materials such as the stores' annual (revenue) records is the appropriate way to obtain this information.

content: Survey store managers for number of customers per day
feedback: Customer counts per day from managers do not reliably identify revenue ranking and surveys add subjectivity.

content: Conduct a focus group with store managers to determine which should be first
feedback: A focus group would collect opinions, but the selection criterion (top revenue) is already fixed by the CEO's decision.

content: Brainstorm with store managers on the current state of each store
feedback: Brainstorming generates ideas; it cannot determine which stores objectively produce the most revenue.

Per BABOK v3, 10.13 Data Modelling, a data model (such as an entity relationship diagram) describes entities of interest - subscriber, medical plan, service provider - and the relationships/associations among them, including cardinality. It is the appropriate diagram for depicting associations between business entities.
A data dictionary (10.12) defines data elements and their attributes but does not diagram relationships between entities.
A use case diagram (10.47) shows actors and their interactions with the system, not associations among data entities.
A data flow diagram (10.14) shows how data moves between processes and stores, not entity associations.

Per BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements, the 'derive' relationship applies when a requirement is derived from another, more abstract requirement - detailed requirements are derived from higher-level business capabilities. The 11 detailed business requirements were elicited to realize the 3 stated capabilities, which is a derivation relationship.
'Depends' describes implementation or value dependencies between requirements, not the abstraction relationship between capabilities and detailed requirements.
'Validates' links requirements to test/validation artifacts that confirm the solution meets the need.
'Verifies' is not the trace relationship between capability statements and the requirements elaborating them.
The requirement is technically achievable, so feasibility is not violated.
The statement is short; brevity (conciseness) is not the issue.
It does not contradict any other requirement, so consistency is not violated.

Per BABOK v3, 7.2 Verify Requirements, an atomic requirement is self-contained and capable of being understood independently of other requirements. Requirement 5 ('select one of the options above and supply corresponding input') cannot be understood without reference to requirement 4 and also bundles two actions, so it violates the atomic characteristic.

content: Inability of a service provider to locate other participating service providers and their fee information
feedback: The capabilities are for subscribers, not for service providers seeking other providers.

content: Inability of a plan subscriber to get directions to nearby participating service providers
feedback: Driving directions are not in scope; only locations, distances, and fee downloads are described.

content: Inability of a plan subscriber to locate and access fee information for nearby participating service providers
feedback: The three scoped capabilities all serve plan subscribers: locating nearby in-network service providers (I, II) and downloading their fee information (III). Per BABOK v3, 6.1/6.2 (needs and business requirements), the primary business need addressed is the subscriber's inability to locate and access fee information for nearby participating providers.

content: Inability of a service provider to be alerted of the location of new plan subscribers in their vicinity
feedback: Nothing in scope alerts service providers about subscribers; the flow is subscriber-initiated.

content: Those identified in the governance approach
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance, the governance approach defines the decision-making process, including who has the authority to approve designs and resolve disagreements when consensus cannot be reached. The final decision therefore rests with those identified in the governance approach.
The BA facilitates decisions and recommends but is not the decision-making authority.
The sponsor may be a decision maker only if designated as such in the governance approach; assuming so bypasses the agreed process.

content: All stakeholders must be in complete agreement
feedback: BABOK does not require unanimous agreement; the governance approach defines how impasses are resolved.

content: Continue the activity with the group and provide a report
feedback: Continuing while the disruption suppresses idea generation defeats the purpose of brainstorming.

content: Reschedule the session without the Director of Human Resources
feedback: Excluding a critical stakeholder would damage engagement and worsen the existing bitterness.

content: Pause the activity and reiterate the rules to the entire group
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 10.5 Brainstorming, ground rules include not criticizing or evaluating ideas during idea generation. When a participant repeatedly violates this, the facilitator should pause the session and restate the rules to the whole group, which corrects the behavior without singling out or alienating the critical stakeholder (see also 9.5 Interaction Skills - facilitation).

content: Ask the Director of Human Resources to observe instead of participate
feedback: Demoting the Director to observer singles them out and risks further alienating a key stakeholder.

content: Compare the expertise of the Director of Human Resources to other stakeholders
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement, stakeholder analysis examines each stakeholder's roles, attitudes, decision-making authority, and level of power or influence - which includes comparing the expertise and influence of the Director of Human Resources relative to other stakeholders.

content: Recommend excluding the Director of Human Resources as a stakeholder
feedback: Excluding a stakeholder the sponsor deems critical is not part of stakeholder analysis and would create risk.

content: Develop a risk plan to anticipate negative behavior from the Director of Human Resources
feedback: Risk planning for stakeholder attitudes is a governance/risk management activity that follows analysis, not part of the analysis itself.

content: Implement all of the Director of Human Resources suggestions to relieve tension
feedback: Implementing all of one stakeholder's suggestions is appeasement, not analysis, and ignores other stakeholders' needs.
A regulator defines external constraints and an operational support manager supports the running solution; neither defines needed tool functionality.

content: Implementation Subject Matter Expert and Tester
feedback: Implementation SMEs and testers advise on design and quality, not on the business need for the tool changes.

content: Domain Subject Matter Expert and End User
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 2.4 Stakeholders, domain subject matter experts have in-depth knowledge of the business area (the product specifications being compared) and end users directly interact with the comparison tool. These two roles are best positioned to explain what changes the tool needs to adequately identify version differences.
The project manager and sponsor manage delivery and funding; they do not use the tool or hold detailed knowledge of its shortcomings.
A communication plan improves information flow but does not reveal duplicated or missing functionality definitions.
A business analysis performance assessment (3.5) evaluates the BA's own work, not the teams' functionality definitions.
A risk register records risks; it does not identify overlapping or missing requirement definitions.

Per BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements, a traceability repository records requirements and their relationships, making it possible to see which functionalities are defined, by whom, and where duplicates or gaps exist. Since multiple teams define the same functionality while other functionality goes undefined, traceability is the approach that exposes and helps resolve these definition issues.
Market analysis examines customers and competitors, which is irrelevant to internal training needs.
Decision analysis compares alternatives against criteria; the task here is defining required changes, not choosing among options.
Root cause analysis diagnoses why a problem occurs, but the cause (no formal materials or training sources) is already known.

Per BABOK v3, 6.2 Define Future State, when the current-state problem is already understood (no written materials, inconsistent shadow-based training), the BA determines the changes required by defining the desired future condition - the goals, capabilities, and resources the training program must provide. Future state analysis identifies what must change to meet the training needs.
Process analysis would improve the training workflow but does not justify the investment to decision makers.

Per BABOK v3, 10.20 Financial Analysis (cost-benefit analysis), demonstrating that the benefits of a formal training program outweigh its costs is how a BA builds the business case needed to gain approval to proceed (see also 6.3 Assess Risks / 6.4 Define Change Strategy where financial justification supports the recommendation).
Business capability analysis identifies capability gaps; it does not provide the financial justification for approval.
Decision analysis compares options; approval to invest hinges on demonstrating benefits versus costs.
No changes to organizational structure, personnel, or culture are recommended.

Per BABOK v3, 8.5 Recommend Actions to Increase Solution Value, one example recommendation is to identify additional capabilities the solution could provide. Requiring an industry selection in the profile to enable industry-specific promotions and personalized search ranking adds new capabilities to the existing solution to increase its value.
The recommendation adds a profile field and personalization; it does not simplify or reduce interfaces.
The site showed strongly positive results; the BA is enhancing the solution, not retiring it.
Use cases and scenarios model actor-system interactions; the BA studied competitors, not interaction flows.

Per BABOK v3, 10.4 Benchmarking and Market Analysis, benchmarking compares the organization's practices against those of similar companies to identify better approaches. Looking into what similar companies did differently to get customers to view promotions is a direct application of this technique.
Observation involves watching users perform work in their environment, which the BA did not do.
Data mining analyzes large data sets for patterns; studying other companies' practices is benchmarking, not data mining.
Accuracy concerns whether the measures themselves are correct, not the gap between expected and actual results.

Per BABOK v3, 8.2 Analyze Performance Measures, comparing actual solution performance against expected or targeted performance identifies performance variance. The sponsor expected at least 30% promotion viewing but observed only 5%, so he was seeking to validate the variance between expected and actual performance of the promotions feature.
Market trends were investigated later by the BA via benchmarking, not what the sponsor was validating.
Risks were not the focus; the sponsor was comparing actual viewing rates against his expectation.

Per BABOK v3, 10.18 Document Analysis, reviewing existing organizational materials - here, the dump of documentation for all 1,000 claims filed during the pilot - to elicit and confirm information is document analysis. By examining the claim documents, the BA discovered that 90% were storm-related property damage claims in one region, identifying the source of the claims increase.
Data mining applies statistical algorithms to large data sets to discover patterns; the BA manually reviewed claim documentation instead.
The survey was used to gather customer perceptions of the site, not to find the source of the claims increase.
Decision modelling represents business decisions and rules; it was not used to analyze the claims.
Reviews (10.37) are used to evaluate work product quality, not to define data elements.
Data mining (10.16) discovers patterns in large data sets; it does not document data element definitions.
A glossary (10.23) defines business terms for a domain, but the data dictionary is the technique specifically for defining data elements and their attributes.

A data dictionary (BABOK v3, 10.12 Data Dictionary) is used to standardize definitions of data elements, including their meanings, allowable values, aliases, and attributes. It is the natural companion document to a class diagram to ensure a common understanding of class attributes.
An activity diagram models workflow/process flow, not causes of a problem.
A data-flow diagram (10.13) shows how information moves through a system.
Cost-benefit is a financial comparison, not a causal diagram.
The Ishikawa diagram, also called a fishbone diagram, is a cause-and-effect diagram used in root cause analysis (BABOK v3, 10.40 Root Cause Analysis) to trace a problem back to its underlying causes.

By analyzing the current state and meeting with senior management to understand goals and how the enterprise currently delivers value, the BA is performing Analyze Current State (BABOK v3, 6.1), whose central output is the business needs. Business needs are the problems and opportunities that guide the change, and any proposed solution is ultimately evaluated against whether it satisfies the business need.
Solution requirements describe capabilities of a specific solution and come later, during Requirements Analysis and Design Definition.
Success measures/metrics are defined when specifying performance measures (7.x/8.1), not by broadly analyzing current state and goals.
Solution limitations are identified while assessing an existing solution (8.3), not during current state analysis of the enterprise.
Procurement needs are not attributes assigned to every activity in an activity list.
Roles and responsibilities are assigned via stakeholder/RACI analysis, not a mandatory attribute of each activity list item.

When building an activity list to plan business analysis work (BABOK, Plan Business Analysis Approach / work breakdown), each activity or task is assigned a unique number (identifier) so it can be unambiguously tracked, estimated, and referenced.
Risk level is assessed via risk analysis and is not required for every task on the activity list.

In BABOK, a distinction is made between work products and deliverables: requirements documentation is a deliverable — an output formally provided to stakeholders. Work products are the documents and notes used during the business analysis process, such as meeting agendas/minutes, interview questions and notes, and presentation slides.
Interview questions and notes are a classic example of a business analysis work product.
Meeting agendas and minutes are work products created during the BA process.
Presentation slides prepared during analysis are work products.
The requirements management plan defines the process for managing scope, traceability, and requirement changes, not communication formality.
The project management plan is the project manager's overall integration document, not the BA communication vehicle.
The scope management plan defines how project scope is defined and controlled, not stakeholder communication of requirements.

The business analysis communication plan describes the stakeholder groups, their communication needs, and the level of formality appropriate for communicating requirements (BABOK, Plan Business Analysis Communication; in v3 this is covered under 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement / 3.4 Plan Business Analysis Governance). It defines how, when, and to whom BA information will be communicated.

content: Make the requirement a lower priority to minimize unanticipated rework and wasted effort
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, stability is a basis for prioritization: requirements that are unstable (likely to change or not sufficiently understood) may be given lower priority to minimize unanticipated rework and wasted effort. The BA should lower the priority until the requirement stabilizes rather than remove it or inflate its priority.

content: Set the requirements at a high priority to adhere to the SME's point of view
feedback: Prioritizing solely on one SME's insistence ignores the stability basis and risks rework on a volatile requirement.

content: Remove the requirement from the project
feedback: Removing the requirement discards a potentially valuable need; instability is grounds for deferral, not deletion.

content: Leave priority unfilled for these requirements
feedback: Leaving priority unassigned fails the prioritization task; every requirement in scope should receive a priority.
Solution cost is the actual estimated/incurred cost of a chosen solution, not a stakeholder-imposed limit.
A budget is the allocated funds for the initiative; here the figure functions as a restriction on acceptable solutions, i.e., a constraint.
Requirements express needs and capabilities; a cost cap restricts the design space rather than describing a needed capability.

A constraint is a restriction or limitation imposed on the solution or the business analysis effort, such as a budget cap. The $300,000 ceiling limits acceptable solution options and is therefore a business constraint (BABOK v3, 6.2 Define Future State — constraints; also discussed in Requirements Analysis and Design Definition).

content: Requirements are not allocated to solution components
feedback: Allocating requirements to solution components (7.2 in Solution Evaluation context / Allocate Requirements) happens after a solution option is selected.

content: Too many approaches are dividing the team
feedback: The number of approaches does not by itself prevent assessment; well-defined options can always be compared.

content: Business needs are unclear to the team
feedback: The scenario states the business need was confirmed by market research and requirements were finalized with the product owner.

content: Requirements and design options are not detailed enough
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 7.5 Define Design Options and 7.6 Analyze Potential Value and Recommend Solution, each design option must be described in sufficient detail to allow comparison and assessment of its expected value. If the team cannot accurately assess the merits of the proposed approaches, the requirements and design options lack the level of detail needed to evaluate them.
A class model shows classes, attributes, and relationships but does not focus on aliases and standardized definitions.
An entity relationship diagram shows entities and their relationships, not aliases and textual definitions of data elements.

A data dictionary (BABOK v3, 10.12 Data Dictionary) records the standard definitions of data elements: their names, aliases, meanings/descriptions, and allowable values. It is the model that captures name, aliases, and description of system data.
A CRUD matrix maps which processes/roles Create, Read, Update, or Delete data — it does not define data elements.

Per BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement, after stakeholder analysis the BA plans stakeholder collaboration; the considerations listed — timing and frequency, location, available tools, delivery methods, and stakeholder preferences — are exactly the collaboration considerations documented in the stakeholder collaboration plan (part of the stakeholder engagement approach).
The business analysis plan covers the overall BA approach and activities, not these specific collaboration details.
'Stakeholder analysis plan' is not a BABOK deliverable; stakeholder analysis has already been completed in the scenario.
The communications management plan is a PMBOK project-management artifact, not the BABOK output for stakeholder collaboration planning.
The BA performance assessment (3.5) measures how well BA work is performed, not how information is organized.

How business analysis information (such as user stories) is organized, stored, and accessed is defined by the information management approach (BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Information Management). Making the stories more accessible means updating how BA information is organized and retrieved, i.e., the information management approach.
Organizational performance standards are enterprise-level benchmarks, not the mechanism for organizing a team's user stories.
A lessons learned register records the insight but does not implement the change in how stories are organized and accessed.
Risk analysis evaluates the likelihood and impact of risks (10.40), not program maturity.
Risk mitigation is a response to reduce risk, not a measurement process.
Risk assessment identifies and evaluates specific risks, not the overall maturity of the security program.

Gap analysis compares the current state against a desired or benchmark state to identify the differences; assessing a security program against a maturity model to determine its maturity level is a gap analysis. In BABOK v3 terms, this is the current state vs. future state comparison performed in Strategy Analysis (6.1/6.2) using benchmarking and gap assessment.

Quality is defined as the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements (ISO definition adopted by BABOK/PMBOK). This underpins verification activities such as BABOK v3, 5.2 Verify Requirements, which assesses the quality characteristics of requirements.
A regulation is an externally imposed rule, not a measure of fulfilling requirements.
A project phase is a segment of the project life cycle.
Scope is the boundary of the solution or work, not the degree of fulfilling requirements.
Interface analysis (10.24) identifies interactions between solutions/systems, not a scope breakdown.
User stories (10.48) capture stakeholder needs in small increments but are not a decomposition of scope into work products.

Functional decomposition (BABOK v3, 10.22 Functional Decomposition) breaks down processes, systems, functional areas, or deliverables into smaller components to help understand and manage the scope of work. It is the technique for decomposing the solution scope into smaller work products.
Scope modelling (10.41) defines the boundaries (what is in/out of scope) rather than breaking the work into smaller parts.
Warranties transfer the cost of failure to the seller/vendor — a transference response.
Performance bonds shift the financial consequence of non-performance to a third party — transference.

Risk transference shifts the impact of a risk to a third party, typically through insurance, warranties, guarantees, and performance bonds (BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management — risk responses). Life cycle costing is a cost-estimating/analysis approach considering total cost of ownership, not a transfer of risk.
Insurance is the textbook example of transferring risk to another party.

The requirements management plan defines the process to be followed in managing the solution scope and requirements, including traceability, attributes, prioritization, and the change/approval process (BABOK — Plan Requirements Management Process; in v3 covered by 3.4 Plan Business Analysis Governance).
The scope management plan is a project management artifact for project scope, not the BA process for solution scope and requirements.
A change management plan addresses organizational or project change control broadly, not the requirements management process itself.
The project management plan integrates all project planning; it does not specifically define the requirements management process.

content: Modify the solution to comply with the policies
feedback: Constraining the solution to legacy policies sacrifices the desired future state value the change is meant to achieve.

content: Identify an assumption about future state value
feedback: Recording an assumption ignores a known, discovered constraint — this is a fact to address, not an assumption.

content: Model two future states, one with and another without these policies
feedback: Modelling two future states defers the decision and adds effort without resolving whether the policies can change.

content: Obtain approval to change the policies
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.2 Define Future State, the BA identifies the conditions (including business policies) needed to meet the business need; when existing policies constrain or are insufficient to achieve the desired future state value, changing those policies becomes part of the change — which, especially in a highly regulated environment, requires obtaining approval from the appropriate authority.

content: Record the concerns in the risk register
feedback: Logging the concern in the risk register tracks it but does nothing to ensure activities are not missed.

content: Schedule a formal review with the stakeholders
feedback: To ensure no performed activities are missed for a high-risk, high-profile process, the BA should have stakeholders formally review the process flow and user stories (BABOK v3, 10.37 Reviews; supports 4.5 Confirm Elicitation Results and 5.5 Verify/Validate). A formal structured review with the people who perform the work is the mechanism to verify completeness and directly address the sponsor's concern.

content: Demonstrate traceability to the business case
feedback: Traceability to the business case shows alignment to objectives, not completeness of captured process activities.

content: Review the final approved elicitation results
feedback: Re-reading already-approved elicitation results would not uncover activities that were never captured.

content: The current state model can help identify opportunities for improvement.
feedback: Identifying improvement opportunities is a stated benefit of analyzing/modeling the current state.

content: The current state model can help validate the solution scope with business and technical stakeholders.
feedback: Validating solution scope with business and technical stakeholders is a valid use of a current state model.

content: The current state model can help the business analyst create Pareto charts for process improvement.
feedback: Current state models (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State) help stakeholders understand how the organization works today, identify improvement opportunities, and validate solution scope against the existing environment. Creating Pareto charts is a data-analysis activity based on measured defect/frequency data, not a purpose of building a current state domain model.

content: The current state model can help assist stakeholders in understanding the current state
feedback: Helping stakeholders understand the current state is the primary purpose of the model.

content: The change-driven approach favors defining requirements through experimentation.
feedback: Experimentation alone is not the BABOK characterization; the emphasis is on team interaction and iterative feedback.

content: The change-driven approach does not define the requirements until after a solution for the problem has been identified.
feedback: Requirements are elicited iteratively throughout, not deferred until after a solution is identified.

content: The change-driven approach favors defining requirements standardized templates.
feedback: Standardized templates and heavy formal documentation characterize the plan-driven (predictive) approach.

content: The change-driven approach favors defining requirements through team interaction.
feedback: The change-driven (adaptive/agile) approach favors defining requirements through team interaction and gathering feedback on working solution increments, with requirements detailed just-in-time (BABOK — Plan Business Analysis Approach; v3 3.1, predictive vs. adaptive approaches).
A risk response plan defines actions for identified risks, not organizational structure and segregation of duties.
Risk analysis assesses likelihood and impact of risks; it does not establish organizational checks and balances.
A risk management plan describes how risk activities will be performed, not the enterprise governance structure over risk.

Risk governance establishes the organizational structures, accountabilities, and checks and balances — including segregation of front, back, and middle office functions — that enable effective oversight of risk management. Governance defines who has authority and how duties are separated, aligning with BABOK v3 governance concepts (3.4 Plan Business Analysis Governance, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management).
Risk identification discovers and lists risks; it does not analyze which areas are most affected.
Risk urgency assessment ranks risks by how soon a response is needed, not by affected area.

Risk categorization groups risks by common causes or by the areas of the project most affected (e.g., using a risk breakdown structure), which helps determine where the project is most exposed. This aligns with organizing risks by source/impact area in risk analysis and management (BABOK v3, 10.40).
Risk analysis broadly evaluates likelihood and impact; categorization is the specific process of grouping risks by affected area.

content: Define the resources that will be needed on the project team
feedback: Per BABOK (Specify and Model Requirements, v3 5.1; modelling concepts), models are simplified representations of reality used to describe situations or problems, define business domain boundaries, and describe thought processes and action flows. Defining the resources needed on the project team is a project management planning activity, not a purpose of a requirements model.

content: Define boundaries for the business domain
feedback: Models such as scope models explicitly define boundaries of the business domain.
Describing a situation or problem is a core purpose of modelling.

content: Describe thought processes and action flows
feedback: Process and activity models describe thought processes and action flows.
Cost budgeting aggregates approved estimates into a cost baseline for a chosen solution, which comes later.
Expert judgment is a means of producing estimates, not the name of this costing process.

content: Rough order of magnitude cost estimating
feedback: Estimating the cost of labor, materials, equipment, and facilities for multiple proposed solution options early in the initiative — before a solution is selected — produces a rough order of magnitude (ROM) estimate, a high-level estimate with wide accuracy ranges used to compare options (BABOK v3, 10.19 Estimation; supports 7.6 Analyze Potential Value and Recommend Solution).
'Scoping the project costs' is not a recognized estimating process in BABOK or PMBOK.
A roles and responsibility matrix maps stakeholders to work, not requirements traceability.
A RACI matrix defines Responsible/Accountable/Consulted/Informed roles, not requirement tracing.

A coverage matrix is a simple table used to manage traceability when there are relatively few requirements or when tracing only high-level requirements (BABOK — Manage Requirements Traceability; v3 5.1 Trace Requirements). For a small project with few requirements, the coverage matrix is the recommended form.
A full requirements trace matrix (often via a requirements management tool) suits larger sets of requirements; BABOK names the coverage matrix for few requirements.
Historical information supports estimation generally but is not the allocation tradeoff consideration BABOK lists.

When assessing tradeoffs between delivery/release options, the BA considers available resources, constraints on the solution, and dependencies between requirements, since some capabilities may need to be delivered together or in sequence (BABOK — Allocate Requirements; v3 7.2 covers allocation and release planning considerations).
