When consolidating input from many groups, the BA ensures the resulting need statement reflects the enterprise perspective.

content: describe the potential solutions that they will satisfy.
feedback: Business needs should be solution-independent; describing potential solutions biases the analysis.

content: have minimal risk associated with them.
feedback: Business needs are statements of problems or opportunities; minimizing risk is not a criterion for defining them.
Plan development occurs after scoping and the business impact assessment provide its inputs.

In the standard business continuity planning life cycle, Scope and Plan Initiation is the first phase: it establishes the scope, objectives, resources, and management support for the BCP effort. Only after initiation does the Business Impact Assessment, plan development, and approval/implementation follow. (General BA/continuity body of knowledge; not a BABOK v3 task.)
The Business Impact Assessment is the second phase, performed after the effort is scoped and initiated.
Plan approval and implementation is the final phase of the BCP process.
At launch the solution barely exists, so there is little to request changes against.

content: Towards the beginning of the project.
feedback: Early in the project requirements are still being defined, so formal change requests are few.

content: During the project scope management processes.
feedback: Scope management processes handle change requests; they are not when requests increase.

content: Towards the end of the project.
feedback: Change requests generally increase toward the end of a project, as stakeholders see the emerging solution and discover gaps or new needs — which is also when changes are most costly. This is why BABOK emphasizes early requirements validation and a defined change control process (BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements and 5.4 Assess Requirements Changes).
Interface analysis (10.24) identifies interfaces between solutions/components, not a visualization of the product.

BABOK v3, 10.36 Prototyping is used to elicit and validate stakeholder needs by providing an early, tangible model of the solution, allowing stakeholders to visualize the final product before further investment. Proof-of-concept and evolutionary prototypes exist precisely for this purpose.
Reverse engineering analyzes an existing system to recover its design, not a future product.
Observation (10.31) studies stakeholders performing current work; it does not show the future product.
Brainstorming (10.5) generates ideas; it does not track issues to resolution.
Observation (10.31) elicits information by watching work being performed.
Interviews (10.25) elicit information from stakeholders; they do not manage issue resolution.

Problem tracking — called Item Tracking in BABOK v3 (10.26) — is the technique for capturing identified issues, assigning ownership, and tracking them through to resolution, ensuring stakeholder concerns and problems raised during business analysis are not lost.
Engagement refers to stakeholder involvement, not a schedule-recovery action.
Preventive actions are taken before a deviation occurs to avoid it; here the variance already existed.

An SPI of 0.87 means the project was already behind schedule; working overtime to bring actual performance back in line with the plan is a corrective action — an action taken to realign performance with the plan after a deviation has occurred (concept used in solution/performance assessment, cf. BABOK v3 Solution Evaluation and 10.28 Metrics and KPIs).
Improvement actions raise performance beyond the plan; here the goal was merely to return to the planned schedule.

Per BABOK (Organize/Specify and Model Requirements; v3 7.1 and 5.1 Trace Requirements), individual requirements may be simple, but complexity arises from the number and nature of relationships and dependencies among requirements. Modeling and traceability structures exist to manage this inter-requirement complexity.
Project management practices are outside the requirements structure itself.
Stakeholders influence elicitation effort, but the stated source of solution complexity is requirement interrelationships.
Cost and schedule constraints affect the project, not the intrinsic complexity among requirements.

BABOK lists 'regulatory or policy compliance' as a basis for prioritizing requirements (BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements; 10.33 Prioritization): requirements that must be implemented to meet regulatory or legal demands are ranked accordingly. Ranking requirements against relevant laws is regulatory ranking.
Business value ranking prioritizes by benefit delivered, not by legal obligation.
Constraint ranking is not a BABOK prioritization basis; constraints are restrictions, not this scheme.
Risk ranking prioritizes by uncertainty/threat of requirements, not by laws and regulations.
Solution design is an output of later work; it does not drive when BA activities are scheduled.
The project manager's experience is not a BABOK factor affecting timing of BA work.
Sponsor interest is not listed as a timing factor; constraints and resource availability are.

BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, element 'Timing of Business Analysis Work' notes that timing is affected by factors such as resource availability, priorities/urgency of the initiative, other concurrent initiatives, and constraints such as contract terms or regulatory deadlines. Of the options, contract terms is the factor BABOK cites.

In the Define Business Case task (BABOK v2 5.5; carried into v3 as the Business Cases technique 10.7), the inputs are the business need, solution scope, stakeholder concerns, and assumptions and constraints. A feasibility study is related pre-project analysis but is not one of the defined inputs to writing the business case.
Stakeholder concerns are a defined input to the business case.
Assumptions and constraints are a defined input to the business case.
The business need is the fundamental input that the business case justifies addressing.
Experiments (prototypes, pilots) take time to build and observe, unsuited to the shortest possible timeline.

BABOK v3, 4.2 Conduct Elicitation distinguishes three types of elicitation: collaborative (direct interaction with stakeholders), research (studying existing sources), and experiments (prototypes, proofs of concept). Given the urgency, the BA worked directly and interactively with the business SMEs — a collaborative elicitation approach, which yields needed information quickly from the people who hold it.
Estimation is a technique (10.19) for sizing effort, not a type of elicitation.
Research relies on studying documents and data sources, slower and less targeted than direct collaboration with the available SMEs.
Validate links a requirement to a test case or acceptance criterion, which is not the case between two features.
Derive applies when one requirement is decomposed or derived from another; F1 and F2 are peer alternatives, not derivations.

BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements defines the relationships derive, depends, satisfy, and validate. F1 (fully charged) and F2 (partially charged) have a dependency relationship: fully charged is effectively the boundary case of the charging capability, so implementing one affects the value/effort of the other (the 'depends' relationship covers necessity and effort subtypes), and neither derives from, satisfies, nor validates the other.
Satisfy links a solution component to the requirement it implements, not two functional requirements to each other.

content: To convey the cost, schedule, and risk information clearly
feedback: Cost, schedule, and risk information belong to project management documents, not the requirements package's purpose.

content: To convey information clearly and in an understandable fashion
feedback: Per BABOK (Prepare Requirements Package, v2 4.4; v3 4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information), the primary goal of a requirements package is to communicate requirements information clearly, in an understandable and usable format appropriate to the audience, so stakeholders can review, understand, and act on it.

content: To present the requirements in packages that are easy for the project team to accomplish in their project execution
feedback: Packaging is for communication and review, not for chunking work for execution.

content: To help the project manager create the work breakdown structure
feedback: The WBS is a project manager's tool; supporting it is not the goal of a requirements package.
Metrics and KPIs define what to measure, not the statistical sampling considerations of how/when to sample.

BABOK v3, 8.1 Measure Solution Performance (element 'Collect Performance Measures') states that when using statistical sampling the BA must consider the volume or sample size and the frequency and timing of measurements, since these determine whether the sample validly represents performance (e.g., calls resolved per hour varies by time of day and season).
Financial analysis evaluates costs and benefits, unrelated to sampling validity.
Benchmarking compares against peers/standards; it is not a sampling consideration.
A business process model shows workflow, useful mainly to domain SMEs, and does not capture the data-centric definition of an insurance product.
An organization model shows structure and roles, irrelevant to product requirements.

An insurance product is defined primarily by its data and business rules (coverages, premiums, policy attributes, relationships), so a logical data model is the requirements viewpoint that both domain SMEs (who validate business meaning) and implementation SMEs (who design the database and system) can work from (BABOK v3, 10.15 Data Modelling; 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements on selecting models for the audience).
A scope model bounds the solution at a high level and lacks the detail SMEs need to review product requirements.

BABOK identifies three categories of stakeholder risk tolerance: risk-aversion, neutrality, and risk-seeking (BABOK v3, 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management, risk attitude/tolerance). Mitigation is a risk response strategy for treating a risk, not a category of risk tolerance.
Risk-aversion is one of the three defined risk tolerance categories.
Risk-seeking is one of the three defined risk tolerance categories.
Neutrality is one of the three defined risk tolerance categories.

content: To define the people that govern decisions in and organization and that define, constrain, or enable organizational policies.
feedback: Business rules analysis defines rules, not the people who govern decisions.

content: To define the job functions, roles and responsibilities, and the designation of power among the project stakeholders.
feedback: Job functions, roles, and responsibilities are the subject of organizational modelling and roles/permissions matrices.

content: To define the rules that govern decisions in and organization and that define, constrain, or enable organizational policies.
feedback: BABOK v3, 10.9 Business Rules Analysis defines the technique as identifying, expressing, and managing the rules that govern decisions in an organization and that define, constrain, or enable organizational operations and policies. Option c restates this definition.

content: To define the historical information that is available for the business analyst to rely on for his research.
feedback: Historical information for research is document analysis territory, not business rules analysis.
'Strategic' measures assess whether objectives and longer-term goals were met, not value versus cost of work products.
'Effectiveness' assesses whether work products were usable as delivered or required extensive revision, not value versus investment.

BABOK v3, 3.5 Identify Business Analysis Performance Improvements (element 'Performance Measures') defines 'significance' as assessing whether the benefit obtained from the work products justifies the cost, time, and resource investment expended to produce them — exactly the measure described.
'Knowledge' assesses whether the BA understood the context and had the skills/experience to perform the work.

In defining the business need (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State), the element 'business goals and objectives' describes the ends the organization is seeking to achieve. Goals are longer-term qualitative statements of desired outcomes, later refined into measurable (SMART) objectives, so they are what is evaluated when determining the ends the enterprise wants to reach.
Alternative identification generates possible means (solution options), not the ends the organization seeks.
Solution assessment evaluates how well a solution delivers value, not the desired ends of the organization.
Requirements are usable representations of needs derived after the business need is defined; they are means, not ends.
188,790 is 435 x 434 without dividing by 2, which double-counts each channel.
189,225 is 435 squared, which is not the channels formula.

Communication channels are calculated with the formula N(N-1)/2, a concept referenced in stakeholder analysis and collaboration planning (BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement, complexity of stakeholder group). With N = 435: 435 x 434 / 2 = 94,395 channels.
435 is just the number of stakeholders, not the number of communication channels among them.

Per BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements (element: Relationships), the 'Satisfy' relationship links a requirement to the solution component or design artifact that implements it. Since the question asks about traceability between a requirement and the solution artifacts implementing it, 'Satisfy' is correct.
'Validate' links a requirement to the test cases or artifacts that verify whether the solution fulfills it.
'Depends' describes effort or value dependency between two requirements, not implementation by a solution component.
'Derive' links a requirement to a higher-level requirement it was decomposed from, not to implementing artifacts.
Stakeholder priority informs prioritization but by itself does not maximize overall solution value across releases.
Available resources are a constraint on allocation, not the basis for maximizing value.

content: Timing of the release and its impact on business
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 7.5 Define Design Options (element: Allocate Requirements), requirements are allocated to releases to maximize business value, and the value delivered depends on when capabilities become available to stakeholders and the business impact of delivering them at that time. Timing of the release and its impact on the business is therefore the basis for maximizing solution value.

content: Components in scope for that release
feedback: Scope components describe what is included, but allocation decisions aim at value delivered, not merely matching components.

content: It is a document that records all change requests for the project.
feedback: It records more than the requests themselves; it tracks their attributes and disposition status over time.

content: It is a document that tracks all unauthorized changes to the project solution.
feedback: The change log tracks all submitted changes, not unauthorized changes.

content: It is a document that tracks all characteristics and status of changes that have been received.
feedback: A change log is a record that tracks the characteristics (date, requester, description, impact) and the current status (e.g., submitted, approved, rejected, deferred) of all changes that have been received. This supports the change control process described in BABOK v3, 5.4 Assess Requirements Changes and 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Governance.

content: It is a document that tracks the changes that have been received, including their implementation to the project solution.
feedback: Not all logged changes are implemented; the log tracks status regardless of whether implementation occurred.

content: Fred will use the appropriate documentation at the discretion of the business analysis team.
feedback: Documentation at the team's discretion characterizes adaptive (change-driven) approaches, not plan-driven ones.

content: Fred will use the project management information system.
feedback: A project management information system is a PM tool, not how business analysis information is captured in a plan-driven approach.

content: Fred will use whatever business analysis forms are most appropriate.
feedback: Using whatever forms seem appropriate reflects informality, which contradicts the plan-driven emphasis on standardization.

Per BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach, predictive (plan-driven) approaches favor formality: information is captured in standardized, formal documentation and templates defined up front, with formal approval gates. Adaptive approaches, by contrast, allow lighter, team-discretion documentation.

content: People who operate and maintain the solution
feedback: People operating the solution are addressed by organizational modelling and roles and permissions matrices, not activity diagrams.

content: Operational business decisions to be made
feedback: Operational business decisions are modelled with decision modelling (e.g., decision tables/trees).

content: Business processes to be performed and managed
feedback: An activity diagram is a form of process modelling (BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Modelling), which visually represents the flow of work. Among the future-state/design elements, it addresses the business processes to be performed and managed by showing sequenced activities, decisions, and parallel flows.
Business policies and rules are captured through business rules analysis, not activity diagrams.
A data flow diagram models data movement through a system, not causes of underperformance.

When a deployed solution underperforms against expected benefits, the BA performs root cause analysis to understand why (BABOK v3, 10.40 Root Cause Analysis, used in 8.5 Assess Solution Limitations / 8.6 Assess Enterprise Limitations). The fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram is a root cause analysis technique for identifying the underlying causes of the performance gap.
Business capability analysis identifies capability gaps for strategy, not the reason an implemented solution misses benefits.
Interface analysis identifies interactions between solutions/users, not root causes of a benefits shortfall.

content: Text message sent via a secured mobile phone
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 4.2 Conduct Elicitation (element: Capture Elicitation Outcomes), acceptable ways to record elicitation results include written documents, visual or audio recordings, and whiteboards/flip charts retained or transcribed. Ad hoc text messages on a phone are not a recognized or reliable form of documenting elicitation outcomes.
Written documents describing outcomes are a standard way to record elicitation results.
Visual or audio recordings are explicitly acceptable capture methods.
Whiteboard content is an acceptable interim record of elicitation results.

content: It's a matrix that uses the Responsible, Accountable, Consult, and Inform tasks as part of the stakeholder analysis process.
feedback: A RACI matrix (BABOK v3, 10.43 Roles and Permissions Matrix; also used in 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement) describes stakeholder involvement using Responsible (does the work), Accountable (ultimately answerable, one per task), Consulted (provides input), and Informed (kept up to date). Option A is the only one reflecting these four designations.

content: It's a role and responsibility chart that identifies when stakeholders are needed in the business analyst duties.
feedback: It describes involvement types per activity, not merely when stakeholders are needed.

content: It's a matrix that uses Role, Action, Consult, and Inform as part of the conduct stakeholder analysis process.
feedback: RACI does not stand for Role, Action, Consult, Inform.

content: It's a rule that the business analyst can use to identify all of the needed stakeholders: roles, actions, communications, and interest.
feedback: RACI is a responsibility matrix, not a rule for discovering all stakeholders.

content: The requirements are interpreted differently by each stakeholder.
feedback: Despite an approved business need and a trusted BA, consensus fails most commonly because stakeholders interpret the (often ambiguous) requirements differently, leading to divergent views of the solution design. BABOK v3 emphasizes shared understanding in 4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information and specifying requirements clearly in 7.1/7.2 to prevent differing interpretations.
Traceability errors would cause coverage/scope gaps, not persistent disagreement in design meetings.

content: The approval authority for the solution is unclear.
feedback: Approval authority affects who signs off, not why stakeholders disagree on the design itself.

content: More solution alternatives need to be generated.
feedback: Nothing indicates the alternatives are insufficient; the issue is lack of shared understanding of what is required.

content: The implementation approach is described in terms of the major features and functions that are to be included.
feedback: Major features and functions describe the solution scope definition element, not the implementation approach.

content: The implementation approach defines major business and technical dependencies that impose constraints to the effort to deploy the solution.
feedback: Business and technical dependencies constraining deployment are the 'dependencies' element, not the implementation approach.

content: The implementation approach describes the new capabilities required to meet the business need.
feedback: New capabilities required to meet the need describe the solution scope/capability definition, not how it will be delivered.

content: The implementation approach describes how the chosen solution approach will deliver the solution scope.
feedback: In defining solution scope, the implementation approach element describes how the chosen solution approach will deliver the solution scope — for example through phased releases, purchasing a package, or outsourcing (BABOK v2, 5.4 Define Solution Scope; in BABOK v3 this thinking is carried into 6.4 Define Change Strategy).
Interviews (even group interviews) are question-and-answer sessions, not intensive multi-day structured working sessions to produce requirements.

A requirements workshop (BABOK v3, 10.51 Workshops) brings carefully selected stakeholders and SMEs together for a short, intensive, facilitated period — typically one to a few days — to define, refine, and document requirements for a solution. It is a highly effective structured elicitation technique.
A focus group gathers attitudes and opinions about a product in an interactive discussion, not to define and document solution requirements.
Prototyping elicits requirements via models of the solution, not through an intensive facilitated stakeholder session.
Scope modeling shows solution boundaries and can reveal affected stakeholders, but it is not the actors/roles starting point described.
Requirements workshops elicit requirements collaboratively; they do not inherently define actors and roles.

content: Scenario and uses cases and user stories
feedback: In stakeholder analysis, scenarios, use cases, and user stories are the technique in which identified stakeholder roles serve as a useful starting point for identifying actors and roles (BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement referencing 10.47 Use Cases and Scenarios and 10.48 User Stories). Stakeholder roles map naturally to the actors in these models.
Interviews gather information from individuals but do not structurally identify actors and roles.
Interviews gather in-depth input from individual stakeholders but cannot measure broad marketplace demand.

A market survey/market analysis (BABOK v3, 10.4 Benchmarking and Market Analysis; 10.45 Survey or Questionnaire) is used to test customer acceptance of a proposed solution and to measure marketplace demand, informing the business case and strategy decisions.
Technology assessment evaluates technical feasibility of options, not customer acceptance or demand.
Prototyping tests usability and validates requirements with users; it does not measure marketplace demand.

Per the BABOK v3 requirements classification schema (2.3) and Strategy Analysis, business requirements — the goals, objectives, and outcomes explaining why the change (central contract management) is needed — must be identified first. They provide the context and basis from which the future state and stakeholder/solution requirements are subsequently derived.
Future state conditions (6.2 Define Future State) are defined based on business requirements/objectives, so they come after.

content: Recommend an off the shelf product
feedback: Recommending an off-the-shelf product is a design/solution decision that would be premature before requirements exist.
An enterprise readiness assessment (8.6/6.4 context) assesses ability to adopt a defined change, which does not yet exist.

Benchmarking (BABOK v3, 10.4 Benchmarking and Market Analysis) compares two or more systems, processes, services, products, or organizational practices against each other or against best-in-class peers to determine the best viable choice and identify improvement opportunities.
Risk identification finds potential uncertain events, it does not compare alternatives.
Risk analysis evaluates likelihood and impact of risks, not comparative merit of systems or products.
Cost-benefit analysis weighs costs against benefits of an option; benchmarking is the comparative technique described.
The project charter formally authorizes a project and names the project manager; its focus is authorization, not describing the solution's positive impact on stakeholders.
The project scope statement describes project deliverables and boundaries, not business needs and stakeholder benefits.
The solution scope defines the capabilities a solution must deliver, not stakeholder identification and impact narrative.

A vision statement articulates the business need, names the key stakeholders it serves, and describes the positive impact the solution will deliver for them — a concise picture of the desired future state. In BABOK v3 terms it summarizes the business need and desired outcomes defined in Strategy Analysis (6.2 Define Future State).
The sponsor authorizes and funds the effort; they do not translate solution scope into project scope.

Once a project is chartered, the project manager is the stakeholder responsible for assessing the solution scope defined by the business analyst and determining the project scope needed to deliver it (BABOK v3, 2.4 Stakeholders — Project Manager role, responsible for delivering the project scope that implements the solution scope).
The BA defines and maintains the solution scope but does not determine the project scope.
Key stakeholders provide input and approvals; converting solution scope to project scope is the PM's responsibility.
An 'analysis task' describes the nature of the work, not its dependency relationships.

An independent task is one that does not rely on any other activity to start and that no other activity relies on to complete. In BABOK v3 planning (3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach) and estimation, understanding task dependencies distinguishes independent tasks from predecessor/dependent tasks when sequencing business analysis work.
A predecessor task is one that other tasks depend on to begin.
A dependent task relies on another task finishing before it can start.
Trial is not part of the SWOT framework.

In SWOT analysis (BABOK v3, 10.46 SWOT Analysis) the letters stand for Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats. The T evaluates external threats — outside factors that could negatively affect the enterprise or initiative.
Test is not part of the SWOT framework.
Time is not part of the SWOT framework.

content: Create a business model canvas to outline business needs
feedback: A business model canvas is a technique to describe the business, not the improvement opportunity itself.

content: Deploy a new system across the companies
feedback: Deploying a new system is a solution choice made prematurely before the opportunity/need is framed.

content: Enhance access to the relevant information
feedback: The problem stated is that disjointed technology impedes gathering the data needed for decision making — an information access problem. Per BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State, a recognized category of improvement opportunity is improving access to information so decision makers get the data they need. Enhancing access to relevant information directly addresses the stated need.

content: Develop a repository of information from each company
feedback: A per-company repository is a specific design option, and separate repositories would not consolidate decision data.

content: Identify a few key stakeholders to resolve the conflicts
feedback: Delegating resolution to a few key stakeholders excludes the sources of the conflicting information and risks bias.

content: Set up additional activities to collaboratively resolve the issues
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results, results from multiple elicitation activities are compared, and variations or inconsistencies are resolved through follow-up elicitation performed collaboratively with the stakeholders involved. Setting up additional activities to collaboratively resolve the differences is the prescribed approach.
Removing conflicting requirements discards potentially valid needs instead of resolving the variance.

content: Weight the results from the observation because it is more reliable
feedback: No elicitation technique's results are inherently more reliable; weighting observation arbitrarily is not supported by BABOK.

content: Compare the Implementation costs of each feature
feedback: Comparing implementation costs presumes the features are valid and understood, which is not yet established.

content: Determine the value of each feature
feedback: Determining value comes after the requests themselves are confirmed as accurate.

content: Prioritize the features in the backlog
feedback: Prioritizing the backlog (5.3 Prioritize Requirements) requires confirmed and understood items first.

The feature suggestions were compiled second-hand from customer support calls, so they are unconfirmed elicitation results. Per BABOK v3, 4.3 Confirm Elicitation Results, the BA must first verify this information for accuracy and consistency with other sources before analyzing it. Only after confirmation can value assessment and prioritization proceed.

content: Identify typical missing or wasteful activities
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 3.5 Identify Business Analysis Performance Improvements, performance measures such as planned vs. actual completion dates and time spent on planned vs. unplanned work are analyzed to identify root causes of performance variances — revealing missing activities that should have been done or wasteful activities that add no value. This converts the anecdotal complaints into evidence-based process improvement.

content: Ensure even workloads of the BA
feedback: Workload balancing is a resource management aim, not the purpose of analyzing deliverable and activity metrics here.
Compensation design is unrelated to business analysis performance assessment in BABOK.

content: Motivate the BA to work belter
feedback: Measurement is for identifying process improvements, not for motivating individual BAs.
19 does not match the difference of the summed phase durations.
51 results from an incorrect shortest total (159 instead of 150).
39 does not correspond to any correct combination of the vendors' totals.

This question references a vendor comparison table (case study of shortlisted vendors for a business intelligence solution). Summing each vendor's implementation phases, the longest implementation is 210 days (30+60+120) and the shortest is 150 days, giving a difference of 60 days. This supports vendor assessment and decision analysis (BABOK v3, 10.49 Vendor Assessment, 10.16 Decision Analysis) when comparing design options.
Scope and requirements prioritization belongs to Requirements Life Cycle Management, not enterprise limitation assessment.
Financial and risk analysis support the business case and recommendations, not the enterprise limitations elements.
Organizational/functional modelling are techniques for describing structure, not the companion elements of this assessment.

BABOK v3, 8.6 Assess Enterprise Limitations includes the elements enterprise culture assessment, stakeholder impact analysis, organizational structure changes, and operational assessment. Alongside the culture assessment, the BA would perform stakeholder impact analysis and operational assessment to understand how the merger affects people, processes, and the organization's ability to operate.

content: Assessed to support benefit management, measurement, and reporting.
feedback: Supporting benefit management, measurement, and reporting describes metrics/KPIs, not decision analysis.

content: Used to assess potential risks that may impact the solution and the cost and benefits associated with it.
feedback: Assessing potential risks and their cost/benefit impact describes risk analysis and management.

content: Forecasts the size of the investment required to deploy and operate the proposed solution.
feedback: Forecasting the size of the required investment describes estimation, not decision analysis.

content: Cost-benefit analysis compares the cost of implementing a solution against the benefits gained. Financial analysis includes the use of financial models that estimate the market value of an organizational asset.
feedback: Decision analysis (BABOK v3, 10.16 Decision Analysis) supports evaluating solution scope and business case options by examining the value of alternatives under uncertainty; in this context it encompasses cost-benefit analysis, which compares implementation costs against benefits gained, and financial analysis using financial models to estimate value (see also 10.20 Financial Analysis). Option D is the only description matching this valuation-of-alternatives purpose.
Describing design options documents the options themselves, not the assignment of requirements to components.

Assigning requirements across solution components (the three sub-projects and the main implementation project) and releases so that overall objectives are met is the Allocate Requirements activity, an element of BABOK v3, 7.5 Define Design Options (Requirements Allocation). Allocation maximizes business value by deciding which components/releases implement which requirements.
Identifying improvement opportunities is part of analyzing options for added value, not distributing requirements.
Defining solution approaches determines the general delivery method (build/buy/etc.), not requirement-to-component assignment.

A scoring system rates each candidate solution against a weighted set of criteria and totals the scores, providing a fast, objective, and fair ranking of alternatives to identify the top-rated solutions. This is an application of decision analysis (BABOK v3, 10.16 Decision Analysis) for comparing multiple solution options.
Acceptance and evaluation criteria define the criteria themselves; alone they do not rank many options quickly.
Vendor assessment evaluates supplier capability and reliability, not the relative merit of many solution options.
A voting system reflects preference or popularity and is less objective and criteria-based than a scoring system.
A resource breakdown structure decomposes only the resources (by category/type), not the program scope itself.
A product breakdown structure decomposes only the product components, not the work and resources needed to create them.
BCWS (planned value) is an earned value cost baseline measure, not a visual decomposition of scope.

A Work Breakdown Structure is a visual, deliverable-oriented decomposition of the total scope of work, breaking scope into work packages that also drive identification of the resources needed to create the defined deliverables. In BABOK v3 this decomposition concept aligns with 10.22 Functional Decomposition, where work and deliverables are broken into component parts for estimation and planning (3.2 Plan Business Analysis Approach).

content: There is no danger; informal communications is a preferred business analysis technique.
feedback: Informal communication carries real risks of lost or ambiguous information; it is not a risk-free preferred technique.

content: The stakeholders may address the business analysis team and the project team directly rather than communicate through Holly.
feedback: Direct stakeholder-to-team communication is a collaboration pattern, not the core documented risk of informality.

content: Stakeholders may miss information and the requirements could become ambiguous.
feedback: Relying solely on informal, undocumented communication risks information being lost, misunderstood, or never reaching all stakeholders, leaving requirements ambiguous. BABOK v3 (3.4 Plan Business Analysis Information Management and 4.5 Manage Stakeholder Collaboration) stresses that business analysis information must be captured and communicated in a form and level of formality that keeps requirements clear, complete, and shared consistently.

content: The stakeholders may not know who's in charge of the solution.
feedback: Confusion over who is in charge is a governance issue, not the primary danger of informal communications.

content: Create the software if you'll keep the solution less than 32 months.
feedback: If kept fewer than 32 months, the vendor option is cheaper, so building would be the wrong choice.

content: Create the software if you'll use the solution longer than 32 months.
feedback: This is a break-even (payback) comparison under BABOK v3, 10.20 Financial Analysis. Building costs $16,000 more up front ($65,000 - $49,000) but saves $500 per month in support ($6,100 - $5,600); $16,000 / $500 = 32 months. Therefore building in-house is financially better only if the solution is used longer than 32 months.

content: There is not enough information to determine which solution is financially better.
feedback: The initial and ongoing costs given are sufficient to compute the 32-month break-even point.

content: Buy from the vendor if you'll keep the solution longer than 32 months.
feedback: Beyond 32 months the vendor's higher monthly support makes buying the more expensive option.
Negotiation is a facilitated agreement approach among stakeholders, not a capacity-per-time-period prioritization basis.
Grouping classifies requirements into related categories rather than by delivery capacity in a time period.

BABOK v3, 10.33 Prioritization describes timeboxing/budgeting as prioritizing requirements based on the amount of work the team can deliver within a set period of time (timeboxing) or a fixed amount of money/resources (budgeting). Since the criterion here is a given period of time, this is time boxing, applied while Planning Business Analysis Governance (3.3).
Budgeting prioritizes against a fixed amount of money or resources, not a fixed period of time.

A control chart is the quality control tool that plots process results over time against control limits to track trends and detect whether execution is in or out of control. This aligns with BABOK v3, 10.28 Metrics and Key Performance Indicators, where trend measurements are used to monitor and manage performance.
A RACI chart assigns roles and responsibilities; it has nothing to do with quality trends.
A PERT chart is a schedule network/estimation diagram, not a quality control chart.
A Pareto chart ranks causes of defects by frequency; it does not track trends over time.

A feasibility study examines multiple solution alternatives and analyzes, with supporting data and statistics, how each could deliver the expected business benefit against the business need. In BABOK v3 this analytical comparison of options supports Recommend Actions to Address Business Needs / Define Change Strategy (6.1-6.4) and is closely related to 10.7 Business Cases and 10.16 Decision Analysis.
A vision statement is a brief aspirational description of the desired future, not a data-driven analysis of alternatives.
A project scope statement defines project boundaries and deliverables, not comparative solution alternatives.
A current state assessment describes the existing environment (BABOK v3, 6.1), not future solution options and their benefits.
An entity relationship diagram models data structures, which are not produced from an initial planning meeting about a delivery process.

At the start of an initiative the BA works with subject matter experts to plan how information will be elicited; per BABOK v3, 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation, the output of this preparatory work is the elicitation activity plan (scope, techniques, logistics, and supporting materials for upcoming elicitation activities). Detailed models of the process come later, after elicitation is conducted and results are confirmed.
A current state flowchart is produced after conducting and confirming elicitation of the process, not from the initial preparatory meeting.
The information management approach is an output of Plan Business Analysis Information Management (3.4), a planning task, not of a meeting with process experts.
A pie chart shows proportional data distribution, not responsibilities.
A line chart shows trends over time, not role assignments.

A RACI chart (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) explicitly tracks the roles and responsibilities of everyone involved in a task. BABOK v3 describes this under 10.39 Roles and Permissions Matrix and uses RACI in 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas for defining stakeholder involvement.
A Gantt chart shows schedule and task durations, not who holds which responsibility type.

The scope statement is the document that communicates, from the program-level perspective, a clear understanding and statement of the technical objectives and the end products, services, or results of the work to be performed. This parallels BABOK v3 solution scope concepts in 6.4 Define Change Strategy and 10.41 Scope Modelling, where scope statements articulate the boundaries and deliverables of the change.
A preliminary scope statement is only an early, high-level draft before the detailed scope is defined.
The program WBS decomposes the scope into work packages; it is a decomposition, not the narrative statement of objectives.
The program charter authorizes the program and names the sponsor; it does not provide the detailed technical scope statement.
A business scenario describes one particular situation or path of usage, not all business activities with their inputs/outputs.

A business process model captures the activities of the business, showing the inputs and outputs of each activity, the sequence of work, and the roles/resources required to perform each activity. This matches BABOK v3, 10.35 Process Modelling (and 10.34 Process Analysis), which describe processes end-to-end with their inputs, outputs, and performers.
Business architecture is a higher-level view of capabilities and structure, not an activity-by-activity input/output model.
A business case justifies an investment (BABOK v3, 10.7); it does not model activities and resources.
The elicitation activity plan is an output of preparation, not the technique for analyzing and mitigating a disruption threat.
Collaborative games foster engagement but do not analyze or mitigate the identified risk of a hostile attendee.
A stakeholder list identifies who is involved; it does not assess or treat the risk of disruption.

A stakeholder likely to react negatively is an elicitation risk. BABOK v3, 4.1 Prepare for Elicitation lists 10.38 Risk Analysis and Management as a technique to identify and manage conditions that could disrupt the elicitation activity, allowing the BA to plan mitigations (e.g., pre-meeting, facilitation tactics) before the workshop.

content: Verify the expected value and its perceived performance
feedback: Verifying expected versus perceived value comes after reliable performance data exists; the data here is not yet trustworthy.

content: Conduct further analysis to protect against false information based on insufficient data
feedback: Two days of observation is a very small sample, and BABOK v3 warns that assessments must guard against drawing conclusions from insufficient or unrepresentative data. Under 7.7 Analyze Potential Value / 8.1 Measure Solution Performance and 10.31 Observation, the BA should conduct further analysis (more observation periods, additional data sources) to validate findings before recommending changes.

content: Propose a change in the organizational chart to give the clerk more responsibilities
feedback: Proposing organizational changes based on two days of observation acts on potentially false conclusions.

content: Benchmark the clerk's performance against other roles and other companies
feedback: Benchmarking against other companies is premature when the internal measurement itself is statistically insufficient.
There is no standard 'scope variance plan'; variances are handled within scope monitoring and control.

The scope management plan defines how the project scope will be defined (created), executed, monitored and controlled, and validated. This governance-of-scope concept parallels BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance, which establishes how scope and requirement changes will be proposed, controlled, and approved.
A staffing management plan addresses human resource acquisition and release, not scope.
A schedule management plan governs the timeline, not scope creation and validation.

Per BABOK v3, 10.34 Process Analysis, inefficiencies arise at points of delay, waiting, and hand-offs between automated and manual steps. The loading stage is reactive: the automated line raises an alarm and then halts until an operator loads 100 cards, so every alarm creates idle time that stops the entire downstream process, making it the most likely source of process inefficiency (a bottleneck).
Packaging is automated and runs continuously, so it introduces minimal variability or waiting.
Sorting is manual but occurs at the end of the line and does not stall upstream stages when it slows.
Printing is automated; its throughput is constrained by loading, not by itself.

content: The newly identified stakeholders will now have to pay for any requirements they want to add to the requirements.
feedback: Stakeholders are not made to pay for their requirements; this is not a recognized consequence.

content: The newly identified stakeholders will need to spend extra time to learn about the project and how it will affect them.
feedback: Extra learning time is a minor inconvenience, not the principal danger to the initiative.

content: The newly identified stakeholders will be excluded from decisions that affect the deliverables that have already been created in the project.
feedback: Late-identified stakeholders are not excluded from future decisions; the real risk is the impact of their new requirements.

content: The newly identified stakeholders may have requirements that require additions to the project or may nullify other requirements already in the project.
feedback: BABOK v3, 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement (and 10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas) warns that failing to identify a key stakeholder means their needs surface late; the newly discovered stakeholders may hold requirements that force additions to the scope or invalidate requirements already elicited and approved, causing rework and change.
Risk-driven is not one of the BABOK approach types; risk tolerance is only one planning consideration.

A change-driven (adaptive/agile) business analysis approach focuses on rapid delivery of business value in short iterations, with requirements detail elaborated just-in-time and higher tolerance for change. BABOK v3, 3.1 Plan Business Analysis Approach contrasts this adaptive approach with the predictive (plan-driven) approach.
'Risk-laden' is not a business analysis approach at all.
'Profit-driven' is not a BABOK-defined approach classification.
A WBS is a scope decomposition tool, not a solicitation document.
A project charter authorizes the project internally; it is not sent to vendors.
A vision document describes the desired future state, not a procurement solicitation.

An invitation for bid (like an RFP, RFQ, or RFI) is a procurement document used to solicit offers from prospective sellers. BABOK v3 discusses soliciting and evaluating vendor proposals in 10.49 Vendor Assessment; the other options are planning or initiation artifacts, not procurement documents.
Functional decomposition (10.22) breaks work or functions into parts but does not model the movement of data.

BABOK v3, 10.13 Data Flow Diagrams describes modelling how data moves through a system: external entities, processes that transform the data, data stores, and data flows, with processes decomposed into progressively lower levels (level 0, 1, 2...) until the system is fully described from input to storage. That is exactly the scenario given.
'Work decomposition' relates to WBS-style breakdown of work, not data movement.
Organizational modelling (10.32) depicts organizational units and reporting lines, not data flows.
Reporting needs concern information/reports produced, not why stakeholders declined to adopt the solution.
Manager-to-staff ratios affect spans of control, not solution adoption behavior.

BABOK v3, 10.32 Organizational Modelling notes that formal org charts do not capture the informal lines of authority, influence, and communication that determine how work actually gets done. The BA validated the solution only against the formal hierarchy; the poor adoption indicates the informal relationships among stakeholders were not considered.
Structural complexity relates to how elaborate the hierarchy is; the BA already conformed to the formal structure.
Define transition requirements addresses capabilities needed to move from the old to the new solution, not value justification.

Examining a proposed solution to determine whether it delivers enough business value to justify implementation is the Assess Proposed Solution task (BABOK v2, 7.1; in BABOK v3 this corresponds to 7.6 Analyze Potential Value and Recommend Solution). It applies whether assessing a single solution's value or ranking multiple options.
Assess organizational readiness evaluates whether the enterprise can adopt the solution, not whether it is worth implementing.
Allocate requirements assigns requirements to solution components and releases, not value assessment.

content: The requirements are categorized by type of work, and then ranked within each category of ease of implementation to difficulty of implementation.
feedback: Categorizing by type of work first is not how the implementation-difficulty basis is defined.

content: The requirements are ranked most difficult to easiest to implement.
feedback: The ranking runs from easiest to hardest, not hardest to easiest.

content: The requirements are ranked from easiest to hardest to implement.
feedback: When prioritizing on the basis of implementation difficulty, requirements are ranked from easiest to most difficult to implement, which helps sequence delivery and often supports early wins and prototyping. This basis for prioritization appears in BABOK v3, 10.33 Prioritization (grounds for prioritization include implementation difficulty) supporting 5.3 Prioritize Requirements.

content: The requirements are categorized by the likelihood of management approving the requirements.
feedback: Likelihood of management approval is a different (political) criterion, not implementation difficulty.

Had the BA analyzed the current state (BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State) and elicited stakeholder needs, the dependency on the existing 'Consolidated Sales by Customer' report would have been identified and preserved when deploying the new report. Understanding the current state and what stakeholders rely on prevents changes from degrading existing value.
Solution assessment results exist only after evaluation; the failure was in not understanding the current state and needs beforehand.
SWOT-style analysis addresses strategic positioning, not a specific functional regression in reporting.
A balanced scorecard measures enterprise performance dimensions, not report-level dependencies.
A decision tree models decision options and probabilistic outcomes (expected monetary value), not variable sensitivity ranges.
An Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram is a root cause analysis tool (BABOK v3, 10.40).
An influence diagram shows relationships among decisions and uncertainties, not ranked sensitivity impacts.

A tornado diagram displays sensitivity analysis results, showing horizontal bars for each variable ordered by the magnitude of their impact on the outcome, which produces the tornado shape. Sensitivity analysis is part of evaluating uncertainty in BABOK v3, 10.16 Decision Analysis and 10.20 Financial Analysis.
A feedback loop is a response mechanism in a system or process, not a spoken conversation on a topic.

A presentation is a structured communication in which one or more speakers address a specific topic for an audience, used to communicate business analysis information such as requirements and designs. This aligns with BABOK v3, 4.4 Communicate Business Analysis Information, which includes presentations (group collaboration) as a means to convey a topic to stakeholders.
A survey/questionnaire (10.45) collects written responses; it involves no speakers.
A formal requirements review (10.37 Reviews) is a structured inspection of work products with defined roles, not simply a conversation on one topic.

content: The needs for the future state solution
feedback: Needs for the future state are defined in Define Future State, using the current state as input rather than being contained in the diagram itself.

content: The cost/benefit measures for the new system
feedback: Cost/benefit measures for a new system come from financial analysis of proposed changes, not the current state diagram.

content: The strengths and weaknesses of the current system
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.1 Analyze Current State, understanding and documenting the current state (e.g., in a current state diagram) reveals how the existing solution works, including its strengths and weaknesses — here, correct billing on actual reads (strength) versus incorrect charges on estimated reads (weakness). This baseline then informs future state discussions (6.2 Define Future State).

content: The change management plan for implementation
feedback: A change management plan belongs to change strategy/implementation planning (6.4), not current state analysis.
Prioritized and approved requirements are one of the three defined inputs.

The Assess Proposed Solution task (BABOK v2, 7.1) has three inputs: assumptions and constraints, requirements (prioritized and approved), and solution option(s). Decision analysis is a technique used within the task to compare options, not an input to it.
Assumptions and constraints are one of the three defined inputs.
Solution options are one of the three defined inputs — the alternatives being assessed.
Benchmarking (10.4) compares practices/performance against peers, not system interoperability.
Interviews elicit stakeholder knowledge but do not systematically analyze system-to-system interfaces.

BABOK v3, 10.24 Interface Analysis is the technique for identifying and defining the interfaces between solution components — software-to-software, system-to-database, and application interactions — including inputs, outputs, and interoperability requirements. It directly addresses whether the databases, software, and web application can work together.
'Usage considerations' is a section within a technique description in BABOK, not an elicitation technique.
A change management system controls how changes are proposed and approved; it does not store all requirements by state.

A repository is the storage mechanism for business analysis information, holding requirements through their lifecycle states — under development, under review, and approved. BABOK v3, 3.4 Plan Business Analysis Information Management describes deciding how requirements and designs will be stored and accessed, with repositories as the standard means.
A project scope statement documents scope boundaries, not a storage method for requirements.
'Requirements register' is not the BABOK term for the storage mechanism; the repository serves this purpose.

content: Change to requirements can occur any time before the project scope statement is created.
feedback: Changes are not confined to the period before the scope statement; they continue after it exists.

content: Changes to requirements can occur at any time.
feedback: Changes to requirements can occur at any time in an initiative; that is precisely why BABOK v3, 3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance requires the BA to plan an approach for how change requests will be proposed, analyzed, prioritized, and approved throughout the lifecycle. No milestone (scope statement, approval, or change control enactment) prevents change from arising.

content: Changes to requirements can occur any time after the project's change control system has been enacted.
feedback: Changes can be requested even before a formal change control system is in place — which is why governance planning is needed early.

content: Change to requirements can occur at any time before the key stakeholders approve the identified requirements.
feedback: Stakeholder approval baselines requirements but does not stop further change requests from occurring.
The project manager manages delivery of scope and schedule but does not approve solution validations or business success criteria.
Subject matter experts provide domain knowledge and input; they have no approval authority.

Per BABOK v3 stakeholder role definitions (2.4 Stakeholders and 9.3 Sponsor/owner responsibilities in classic CBAP prep material), the solution owner is the business-side authority accountable for the solution and therefore approves scope statements, phase gate reviews, solution validations, scope changes, and success criteria. This approval authority for the solution's business outcomes rests with the owner of the solution, not with delivery or advisory roles.
'Stakeholder' is a generic term covering all interested parties, not the specific approving role.
The project manager needs requirements information for scoping, estimating, and risk, not detailed technical interface specifications.
Regulators need evidence of compliance with laws and standards, not technical interface detail.
Testers need requirements detailed enough to be testable, but the design-level technical interface detail is primarily for those building the solution.

Per BABOK v3 (2.4.8 Implementation Subject Matter Expert; also 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement / 4.5 Communicate BA Information), implementation SMEs design and build the solution, so they require the most detailed technical requirements, including interface requirements that describe how the solution interacts with other systems.

content: Dented because of the company’s risk aversion
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 10.40 Risk Analysis and Management, stakeholder risk tolerance (risk-averse, neutral, or risk-seeking) shapes how an organization responds to risk. A conservative company with rigorous risk controls, restricted access, and on-site-only maintenance is risk-averse, so it would deny a request for 24-hour remote access to production data because the request conflicts with its risk appetite.

content: Accepted, because the company has an urgent problem to solve
feedback: Urgency does not override an established risk-averse posture and internal audit controls.

content: Denied- because the vendor requested it
feedback: The denial is grounded in risk aversion and control policy, not simply in who made the request.

content: ccepted, because immediate remote access will resolve any issue
feedback: Accepting unrestricted remote access contradicts the company's stated access-restriction and on-site maintenance controls.
Network topology mapping is an IT infrastructure activity, not a BABOK business analysis technique, and does not analyze how information is processed and stored.
A data dictionary/glossary (10.12) defines data elements and terms; it does not map information flow between systems.

Per BABOK v3, 10.13 Data Flow Diagrams, DFDs show how information is input, processed, stored, and output across systems, processes, and external entities. Mapping systems and their purposes specifically to analyze the input-process-store-output flow of information is data flow diagramming.
Prototyping (10.36) creates early models of a solution's interface, not a map of information flow across existing systems.

content: It identifies new capabilities required by the organization to meet the business need.
feedback: In BABOK v3 Strategy Analysis (6.2 Define Future State, corresponding to the classic 'Assess Capability Gaps' task), the purpose is to identify the new capabilities the organization requires in order to meet the business need, by comparing current capabilities against those needed for the desired future state.

content: It identifies the causal factors that are contributing to an effect the solution will solve.
feedback: Identifying causal factors is root cause analysis, part of analyzing the current state/business need, not capability gap assessment.

content: It describes the ends that the organization wants to improve.
feedback: Describing the ends the organization wants to improve is the definition of business goals/objectives, not the capability gap task.

content: It identifies the skill gaps in the existing resources.
feedback: Capability gaps concern organizational capabilities (processes, functions, technology, staff competencies as a whole), not merely skill gaps of existing resources.
The stakeholder engagement approach (3.2) addresses collaboration and communication with stakeholders, not requirement attributes.

Per BABOK v3, 3.4 Plan Business Analysis Information Management, the information management approach defines how business analysis information, including requirements, will be captured and organized, and it specifies the set of attributes (e.g., ownership, source, priority, complexity, stability) to be used for each requirement or set of requirements.
The business analysis approach (3.1) defines the overall methodology and activities, not the attribute set for requirements.
The governance approach (3.3) defines decision-making, prioritization, and change control processes, not requirement attributes.
Using a template architecture concerns standardized documentation formats and would not by itself prevent missing or contradictory content.
Solution scope development is a Strategy Analysis concern; the gap here is in requirement quality, not scope definition.
Accommodating viewpoints relates to modeling requirements from different stakeholder perspectives, not to eliminating gaps and contradictions.

Per BABOK v3, 7.2 Verify Requirements, verification ensures that specified and modeled requirements meet quality characteristics including completeness and consistency (7.2.4 Elements: Characteristics of Requirements Quality). Missing, inconsistent, and contradictory requirements discovered during a presentation indicate the BA failed to verify the requirements, i.e., failed to ensure completeness and consistency.
Transition requirements are temporary by definition and cease to be relevant once the change is complete (BABOK v3, 2.3).
Stakeholder requirements express the needs of particular stakeholders in a specific change context and are less transferable.
Business requirements state goals of a specific change initiative; while high-level, the ongoing quality/regulatory obligations cited by BABOK as reusable are non-functional in nature.

Per BABOK v3, 5.5 Maintain Requirements, requirements that are candidates for reuse are those the organization must satisfy on an ongoing basis, such as regulatory requirements, contractual obligations, quality standards, and service level agreements — which are typically expressed as non-functional requirements. These remain applicable across multiple change initiatives.
Elicitation draws out information from stakeholders; it does not itself define business problems and opportunities.
Solution assessment and validation evaluates proposed or deployed solutions, which do not yet exist here.

In-depth analysis and documentation of business problems and opportunities maps to Enterprise Analysis (BABOK v2 knowledge area, equivalent to Strategy Analysis in BABOK v3), which defines the business need, analyzes the current state, and identifies opportunities for the organization.
Requirements management and communication manages and communicates approved requirements; it does not analyze business problems.

Requirements describe WHAT the organization or solution must do or deliver, deliberately leaving the 'how' to design. In organizing and modeling requirements (BABOK v3, 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements), each requirement answers the interrogative 'what' — what capability, condition, or need must be satisfied.
'When' relates to scheduling and prioritization, which are planning concerns, not the essence of a requirement statement.
'Why' is answered by business goals and the business need, which provide the rationale behind requirements.
'How' is answered by design and solution options, which requirements should intentionally avoid prescribing.
A data dictionary is a technique for defining data elements, not a prioritization factor.
A constraint is a limitation imposed on the solution (e.g., budget, regulation), not an implementation-order relationship between requirements.

Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements (5.3.4 Elements: Basis for Prioritization), dependencies are a recognized basis: a requirement may be given high priority because other requirements depend on it and it must be implemented first. 'Parsing of data' being prerequisite to other requirements is a dependency-driven prioritization.
Risk-based prioritization addresses uncertainty or threat, not the sequencing need described here.
Transition requirements are temporary and lose relevance once the implemented change is complete.
Solution architecture is project- and solution-specific and is a technical design artifact, not a BA artifact for enterprise-wide reuse.
A high-level business requirements document is tied to the goals of one change initiative rather than ongoing organizational use.

Per BABOK v3, 5.5 Maintain Requirements, business analysts maintain requirements and designs for long-term reuse across the enterprise; process models (10.35) describing the organization's ongoing business operations are artifacts that remain accurate and useful organization-wide after a project ends. They document how the business works, independent of the specific initiative.

Per BABOK v3, 6.3 Recommend Actions to Increase Value and 10.7 Business Cases, a business case captures the need, expected benefits (the $1M+ savings), costs, impacts on stakeholder groups, and risks, and is the standard artifact used to justify a recommended change to management for approval. Before presenting a recommendation of this magnitude, the team should first create the business case.
An organizational model describes structure and roles; it does not justify the investment decision.
The change strategy is defined after the recommendation/business case establishes that the change is worth pursuing.
Validating cost savings is part of building the business case, not a standalone first step preceding it.
A cause-and-effect (fishbone) diagram organizes potential causes of a problem; it shows no time sequence.
A histogram shows the frequency distribution of data, not order of occurrence over time.

A run chart is a line graph plotting data points in time order to show trends, variation, and improvements or declines in a process over time; it is the tool used for trend analysis. In BABOK v3 this aligns with the metrics/KPI monitoring concepts (10.28 Metrics and KPIs) and classic quality tools.
A Pareto chart ranks categories by frequency/impact to highlight the vital few causes, not trends over time.
No requirement change has been justified; updating requirements does not address the stakeholder's doubt about value.
Merely recording the feedback and moving on ignores the stakeholder's attitude and risks losing their support.

Per BABOK v3, 6.2 Define Future State, the future state description articulates the expected value and benefits of the change. When a stakeholder doubts the solution's value, reviewing the future state (goals, expected benefits, and how the change delivers value) addresses the concern directly and helps maintain engagement and support (see also 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement on managing attitudes toward the change).
The governance approach concerns decision-making and change control processes, not resolving a stakeholder's value concerns.
The BA determined it is a genuine requirement change, so closing it as 'working as designed' would ignore the needed assessment.

content: Evaluate the risk analysis and management information
feedback: Risk information may inform the assessment but does not identify what the change affects; impact analysis via traceability does.
Root cause analysis diagnoses underlying causes of problems; the cause is already known — the requirement changed.

content: Conduct an impact analysis using traceability
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 6.1 Assess Requirements Changes (RLCM) and 5.1 Trace Requirements, a proposed change to a requirement is assessed through impact analysis, using traceability to identify all related requirements, designs, and solution components affected by the change (see also 10.5.3 Impact Analysis under Backlog/traceability discussions and 5.1.5 Traceability for impact analysis).

The project charter is the document that formally authorizes a project to exist within the organization and gives the project manager authority to apply resources. In CBAP prep material this aligns with initiation outputs following enterprise analysis/strategy analysis (business case feeding the charter).
A network diagram sequences project activities; it authorizes nothing.
Project integration management is a coordination discipline, not an authorizing document.
Project boundaries define what is in and out of scope, not the project's authorization.

content: You may need to determine the cost of each requirement.
feedback: Cost is one possible input to prioritization, not the purpose of prioritizing.

content: You may need to determine the schedule for each requirement.
feedback: Scheduling implementation is a planning consequence, not the reason BAs prioritize requirements.

content: You may need to determine which requirements are most critical so the analysis and implementation efforts focus on the most critical requirements.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, prioritization ranks requirements by relative importance and value so that analysis and implementation effort is focused on the most critical, highest-value requirements first, especially given limited time and resources.

content: You may need to determine which requirements are most critical so additional risk analysis can be completed on those requirements.
feedback: Prioritization is not performed primarily to select requirements for additional risk analysis; risk is just one basis for prioritization.
The sender encodes and originates the message but does not transfer it between entities.

In the communication model referenced by CBAP prep material (aligned with BABOK v3 underlying competency 9.5 Communication Skills), the medium (channel) is the component that carries or transfers the message between the sender and one or more receivers.
The decoder translates the received signal back into meaning; it does not transport the message.
The receiver accepts and decodes the message at the destination; it is not the transfer mechanism.
A Pareto chart ranks frequency/impact of categories to focus effort but does not itself trace causes to their root; it is not the technique BABOK names for root cause analysis.
A data-flow diagram models movement of information through a system, not causes of a problem.
A Gantt chart displays project schedule activities over time, unrelated to causal analysis.

Per BABOK v3, 10.40 Root Cause Analysis, the fishbone (Ishikawa, cause-and-effect) diagram is one of the primary techniques used to identify and organize the possible underlying causes of a problem, along with the five whys.
'Achievements' is not a defined planning term for zero-duration progress markers.

Milestones (misspelled 'Millstones' in the option) are significant point-in-time events with zero duration ('timeless') that mark progress, typically achieved at the end of a phase in the project life cycle. BABOK v3 references milestones in planning and estimation contexts (e.g., 10.19 Estimation, 10.35-related planning discussions).
Phases are spans of work with duration, not point-in-time events.
Templates are reusable document formats, unrelated to schedule events.
Changing the business process is a larger intervention that is not justified merely by a low-frequency feature.
The requirement still represents a valid, recurring business scenario, so removing it outright discards legitimate value.

content: ssign it a lower priority for future release
feedback: Per BABOK v3, 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, priority is based on factors such as benefit, cost, and frequency of use. Features used rarely (three times a year) by a small group but requiring high implementation effort have low value relative to cost, so the BA should recommend assigning them a lower priority and deferring them to a future release rather than discarding a legitimate need.

content: Deliver as part of the initial release
feedback: Delivering high-effort, low-frequency features in the initial release misallocates limited resources away from higher-value requirements.

In Plan Business Analysis Approach (BABOK v3, 3.1; BABOK v2, 2.1), process modeling is listed as a technique used to define and document the business analysis approach itself — the business analysis process can be modeled as a process to communicate how BA work will be performed.
A structured walkthrough is used to review/validate the approach with stakeholders, not to define and document it.
Decision analysis helps rate and select among approach options, not document the chosen approach.
Control charts are a quality/performance monitoring tool and are not a technique of planning the BA approach.
A RACI chart maps stakeholder responsibility levels (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) for tasks, not information flows between stakeholders.

A communications requirements matrix is a table of all stakeholders that maps who contributes or provides information to whom, including the type, format, and frequency of communications. This supports BABOK v3 tasks 3.2 Plan Stakeholder Engagement and 4.5 Communicate Business Analysis Information.
The project scope statement defines scope and deliverables, not stakeholder communications.
An organizational breakdown structure arranges the organization's units hierarchically; it does not map information contributions.
A feasibility study assesses whether a proposed solution is viable overall, not a measured head-to-head comparison of materials.
A stress test evaluates behavior under extreme load for a single item; the scenario is about comparative evaluation.
Alternative identification generates possible options; here the alternatives are already identified and are being compared.

Per BABOK v3, 10.4 Benchmarking and Market Analysis, benchmarking involves comparing practices, processes, or products/materials against each other or against standards by testing and measuring results to determine which performs best. Testing two materials and comparing measured results to select the better one is a benchmarking comparison.
Tracing to stakeholder tasks shows who does what but not where requirements collide in the solution.
Requirements already trace conceptually to business needs and each looked valid in isolation; the conflicts emerge at the implementation (component) level.
Organizational functions describe structure, not the implementation elements where the contradictions materialize.

Per BABOK v3, 5.1 Trace Requirements, requirements can be traced forward to solution components, and traceability relationships help detect conflicts. When independently approved requirements are allocated to the same IT systems/solution components, tracing requirements to solution components reveals early that different requirements impose contradictory demands on the same component — exactly the conflicts that surfaced at implementation.
Progressive elaboration is the iterative refinement of plans/requirements over time, not an elicitation observation approach.
Shadowing in the passive sense means watching without interfering; Martha is participating in the work.
'Cross training requirements elicitation' is not a BABOK technique.

Per BABOK v3, 10.31 Observation, the active/participatory approach involves the observer engaging with and participating in the work while observing, asking questions and even helping perform the tasks to understand the process. Martha working alongside Sarah and helping complete the work is active (participatory) observation.

content: It determines how requirements will be prioritized based on the most important stakeholders to the least important stakeholders.
feedback: Prioritization ranks requirements by value, risk, cost, and dependencies, not by ranking stakeholders' importance.

content: It is only used with the change-driven approach to business analysis.
feedback: Prioritization applies in change-driven approaches (e.g., backlogs) but is not exclusive to them.

content: It is only used with the plan-driven approach to business analysis.
feedback: Prioritization is also central to plan-driven approaches but not exclusive to them either.

content: It determines how requirements will be prioritized and how those priorities will be used to define the solution scope.
feedback: Per BABOK v3, planning prioritization (3.3 Plan Business Analysis Governance) and 5.3 Prioritize Requirements, requirements prioritization establishes how requirements will be ranked by relative importance and how those priorities are used to determine which requirements are included in the solution scope and delivered first.
Organizational modeling (10.32) is a listed technique for modeling requirements about roles and structures.
Data modeling (10.11) is a listed technique for modeling information concepts and relationships.

Per BABOK v3, 7.1 Specify and Model Requirements, modeling techniques include organizational modeling (10.32), data modeling (10.11), and state modeling/state diagrams (10.44). Work breakdown structure creation is a project management decomposition technique for organizing project work, not a BABOK technique for specifying or modeling requirements.
State modeling/state diagrams (10.44) is a listed technique for modeling entity life cycles.

In the basic communication model (aligned with BABOK v3 underlying competency 9.5 Communication Skills), the receiver decodes — i.e., translates — the transmitted message back into understandable meaning. With 'decoder' not offered, the receiver is the component that performs the translation of the message.
The medium merely carries the message between parties; it performs no translation.
'Translator' is not a defined component of the communication model; it restates the question rather than naming a model element.
The sender encodes the original idea into a message; decoding/translation happens at the receiving end.
